description: Stated differences not recorded in the candidate's own words.
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
Presbytery granted a doctrinal exception that may be hostile to our system of doctrine ( WCF 1.1-10). More information or clarity is needed on the exception.
responses: The Candidates expressed view is that, the
gifts of tongues and prophecy, though no longer normative,
still occur in a manner that can be defined by such terms.
There have been individuals who have reportedly received, in
a miraculous manner, the gift of knowing (speaking and/or
reading) a language they have had no previous experience or
training in. He believes these gifts can be given as a means of
grace from God and would categorize this in the realm of the
“gift of tongues”. Also, individuals may at times be blessed
with extraordinary insight on a certain issue for which they
may have not previously demonstrated any particular
knowledge, expertise or have no training in. The Candidate
defines such situations as being in the realm of the “gift of
prophecy”, using its wider definition. “Prophecy” being
looked at as per 1 Corinthians 14:3-4 as speaking to people
for their upbuilding and encouragement and consolation…
(to) build up the church.” It would be defined as: “the gift of
communicating and enforcing revealed truth”. Though such
situations could also come under the definition of being gifts
of “wisdom”, “knowledge”, and/or “discernment”, the
definition of “prophecy” as an over-arching term for such is
also acceptable for such circumstances. The Candidate agrees
that “prophecy” defined as new revelation from God to a
person that is “extra-Biblical” or outside of God’s revealed
and complete Word, does not exist.
Presbytery does not acknowledge any
biblical support for the continuation of such gifts, nor does it
provide any rationale as to how a person can meaningfully
differentiate between modern day “prophecy” and Scripture.
The candidate affirmed that the supreme
judge by which all controversies of religion are to be
determined, and all decrees of councils, opinions of ancient
writers, doctrines of men, and private spirits, are to be
examined, and in whose sentence we are to rest, can be no
other but the Holy Spirit speaking in the Scripture (Mat 22:29;
Presbytery did not address the issue the
Assembly took with its previous response. Presbytery
provides no “biblical support for the continuation of such
gifts, nor does it provide any rationale as to how a person can
meaningfully differentiate between modern day “prophecy”
and Scripture,” in its response. Further, its response is merely
a citation of WCF 1.10, and so provides the Assembly with no
new information, as the candidate stated no difference with
that portion of the Standards.
We agree that TE [Name Omitted]’s
views in this matter are concerning and contrary to our
Standards. We regret approving this exception and we resolve
not to grant such an exception in the future as we believe this
exception to be hostile to our system of doctrine. TE [Name
Omitted] has been encouraged to transfer his ministerial
credentials to a more suitable ecclesiastical body, as he is
currently “without call” and we are shepherding him along in
the process. We expect that this will be done before the Fall
2025 stated meeting of the Canada West Presbytery.

description: Presbytery granted a doctrinal exception that appears to be out of accord with the fundamentals of our doctrinal standards. Candidates stated difference, " WLC 177 – worthy participation of the Lord’s Supper I am more than willing to submit in teaching and practice to the confessional position of requiring a public profession of faith from covenant children before admitting them to the Lord's Table. I am strongly against Federal Vision and I am intentionally distancing myself from such distinctive teachings in the CREC (to which I never agreed). However, it seems to me that, within the context of faithful family nurture and given the place of covenant children in both the old and new covenants, we should expect that our children already possess the prerequisite faith from the earliest age, and that they will gradually, organically, display this faith in an ageappropriate manner. While we do not know whether our children are elect or not, and we cannot peer infallibly into their hearts, I think we should treat them with the judgment of charity expressed, for instance, in Canons of Dordt 1.17 and Luke 18:15ff, teaching them to pray "Our Father in heaven" and to sing "Jesus loves me" in the full sense of the words. Nevertheless, as I said, I am very happy to abide by the confessional requirements of a public profession of faith ( WLC 177; DFW 56- 4.j) and have already spoken with my own children about doing so. To administer the Supper and fence the table in this way would pose no harm to my conscience whatsoever.".
responses: TE [Name Omitted] has submitted to and
upheld our constitutional standards in every way, both in
teaching and in practice, and we are confident that he will
continue to do so in keeping with his ministerial vows. His
private views in this matter are a commonly held exception
within the PCA and he holds them with the utmost humility
and discretion. We believe that, in granting such an exception
and requiring him not to teach or practice his views, we have
done nothing contrary to the longstanding precedent and
current practice of the PCA.

description: Minutes show the Presbytery examined the candidates in biblical counseling. While this may be prudent, it is not an area of examination for ordination according to the BCO, nor does Presbytery include it in its Standing Rules.
responses: In recent years, the Presbytery has
adopted the practice of having its Credentials Committee
examine candidates in the area of Christian Counseling. We
recognize that it is not a required area of examination, but
because it is done it is reported as a part of our minutes. In the
future the minutes will clarify that such exam is extracurricular to the BCO requirements and are not a part of the
Ordination qualifications.

responses: CVP acknowledges that it failed to follow

exception “was not out of accord with any fundamental of our

to be more diligent to use the prescribed wording in dealing

description: Four timely and orderly complaints dismissed without a hearing.
responses: CVP responds by noting that these four
complaints were in fact adjudicated by a Commission but the
Commission was unable to proceed after reviewing the
complaints, finding all four complaints to be “out of order
administratively,” in that they were not filed against an action
of a court ( BCO 43-1). CVP was also notified by the
Complainant’s civil attorney that the Complaiant desired to
withdraw his complaints and to receive no further interaction
with CVP concerning these matters.
No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( RAO 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaints. The complaints
were, in fact, against actions of a court ( BCO 43-1), and it was
improper to dismiss the complaints as “out of order
administratively” on the stated grounds without affording the
Complainant the right of a hearing. A purported subsequent
withdrawal of a complaint could dispose of a complaint only
after an unconstitutional action is rescinded.
CVP responds by acknowledging that it
erred in that it should first have determined that the matter was
“administratively out of order” before forming a commission
( BCO 43.8). The commission also erred in that it was
improper to dismiss one complaint as “out of order
administratively” on the stated grounds given. The wording in
the commission’s report admits to some adjudication of the
complaint, which was out of order and was not a strict
judgment based on BCO 43:1. CVP affirms to the General
Assembly that it will follow the order found in BCO 43:8, and
it will follow the order and steps used by the SJC found in
OMSJC 9.1 and 9.2. Moreover, CVP affirms that it will seek
to provide the proper grounds for any ruling in future cases.
CVP adds that it was notified by the Complainant’s civil
attorney that the Complainant has withdrawn his complaints
and desires no further interaction with CVP concerning these
matters. The Complainant is no longer a member of any
congregation of the PCA. This has the effect of rendering the
Complaints administratively out of order in the same fashion
as the SJC acted in case SJC 2022-15 ( RE Terry Murdock vs.
South Florida Presbytery ) and SJC 2022-15 ( Mr. Peter
Benyola vs. Central Florida Presbytery ).
No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( RAO 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaints. The complaints
were made by a member in good standing of the PCA at the
time of filing and at the time of the adjudication of the
complaints. Furthermore, the complaints were against actions
of a court ( BCO 43-1). Therefore, it was improper to dismiss
the complaints as “out of order administratively” on the stated
grounds without affording the Complainant the right of a
hearing. a purported subsequent withdrawal of a complaint
could dispose of a complaint only after an unconstitutional
action is rescinded. (See the Response of Northwest Georgia
Presbytery to exception of substance 2023-01 for a
satisfactory response to the same error.)

description: Incomplete record of review of records of church.
No record commissioners appointed by the church presented and prosecuted the call of a TE before Presbytery.
responses: Agreed that there is no explicit record of
named commissioners actually presenting and prosecuting the
call. But there is record that the call was reviewed and the
terms were approved, which surely assumes it was presented
and prosecuted. Previous “Guidelines to Exceptions” do not
list the failure to explicitly state that named commissioners
presented and prosecuted the call as an “exception of
substance.” In the future, will this level of detail qualify under
RAO 16-6.c.1) as an “Exception of Substance”? Will this be
considered a “serious irregularity” or “important
delinquency”?
Presbytery must respond following the
pattern of RAO 16-10.b.

pattern of RAO 16-10.b.

description: No record that members of the provisional Session were called by the congregation.
responses: Presbytery confirms that the provisional
(temporary) session was not called by the congregation. The
church had particularized on October 4, 2020, with a session
comprised of a pastor and two Ruling Elders. As of the
January 28, 2023 Presbytery meeting, both of the Ruling
Elders had resigned and left the church. Since there were no
Ruling Elders, Presbytery’s judgment was that the church
reverted back to mission status. Therefore, per BCO 5-3,
Presbytery appointed a temporary session.
Presbytery accepts RPR’s judgment and promises to
be more careful in the future. For corrective action, the
Presbytery will advise the church membership regarding a
congregational meeting and proceed accordingly.

Of the 36 churches in Presbytery, 11 have no mention of any review of their minutes and 10 have evidence of reviews of prior year's minutes, but not of 2023 minutes. Further, there is no record that Presbytery required responses to exceptions of substance.

— TEs are transferred to other PCA presbyteries with no record (in one case) of a call or other reason for the transfer, and no evidence that the action is pending the action of the other court, that a certificate of dismission was issued, or, from subsequent minutes, that the man transferred at the September meeting was received by the other presbytery.

A TE is received from another PCA Presbytery with the understanding that the other Presbytery will issue a certificate of transfer. There is no record in subsequent minutes that the transfer certificate was ever received, and the TE was installed without reference to the certificate having been received.

( BCO 32; BCO 31-2; BCO 34; BCO 38-1; RONR (12th ed) 48:14, 35:13) — Presbytery votes to "strike from the minutes of its 86th Meeting of the Presbytery [which would have been over 3 years prior] where it erroneously records that TE [Name omitted] was suspended from the sacraments." Presbytery then votes to instruct the Stated Clerk to "communicate the judgment of CPJC" to the TE, which judgment was the censure of deposition for the sin of failing to manage his household well. There is no record of the 2/3 vote needed to amend previous minutes. Further, there is no record of any formal process, of a case without process, or of any report as to how this matter came about. It is not clear if this is a new censure or if it is imposing what Presbytery intended to impose at the 86th meeting. Either is constitutionally dubious at this point.

( BCO 21-5; BCO 15; BCO 21-6; BCO 21-9) — Two installation commission reports are attached to the minutes. The first does not show attendance, the order of service, or most of the required elements of BCO 21-5. The other omits all the elements of the first paragraph of 21-5. Further, the second report sometimes refers to the TE as being installed as an Associate Pastor and sometimes as an Assistant Pastor.

— A TE from some other body (it is not specified whether he is from another PCA Presbytery) is examined for licensure to serve as stated supply at a church. There is no record the Session made a request for stated supply or that the other body consented to have the TE serve within the bounds of Chesapeake Presbytery.

Further, these is no record of reports from seminary instructors for candidates still in seminary.
Presbytery approved a candidate for licensure whom they judged held a view that was hostile to the system or striking at the vitals.
responses: We appreciate the RPR's diligence and
recognize Presbytery's action was irregular, though we
believed it to be appropriate in this case. We would offer the
following rationale as to why we made this decision: 1) The
candidate was already serving as a pastoral intern at one of
our churches and was seeking to fulfill all of the formal
internship requirements of BCO 19-7ff, which includes the
testing of his preaching and teaching gifts. In order for the
intern to do this adequately, we believed licensure would be
necessary. 2) At our Presbytery meeting in March, members
of the Session where the candidate was serving as a pastoral
intern made clear that they agreed with Presbytery with
respect to the assessment of the candidate's particular view on
remarriage, and that they would be working with the candidate
to encourage him to reconsider his view in light of Scripture
and the standards and that they would ensure he not teach or
preach this view to the congregation. 3) The candidate seemed
humble and teachable and so we believed giving some
additional time to reconsider the view while working closely
with the Session of the church where he was serving and
fulfilling his internship requirements would be the kindest and
most reasonable approach. 4) At the end of the examination
on the floor of Presbytery, it was made clear to the candidate
by the chair of the Leadership Development Committee that
unless the candidate's view changed to be in accord with our
system of doctrine, he would not be able to be ordained in our
Presbytery. 5) As noted in the minutes, we also instructed the
candidate that he "not teach, preach, or counsel his exception
to the standards."
BCO 19-2.f is explicit that “The court
may grant an exception to any difference of doctrine ONLY
if in the court’s judgment the applicant’s declared difference
is not out of accord with any fundamental of our system of
doctrine because the difference is neither hostile to the system
nor strikes at the vitals of religion.” (emphasis added) This
language does not give Presbytery the authority to grant an
exception that is hostile to the system of doctrine or strikes at
the vitals, even if Presbytery believes there are special
circumstances.

description: No record of 2/3 vote to divest TE.
responses: Covenant Presbytery respectfully
disagrees that it erred in this citation. RPR alleges there was
no record of 2/3 vote to divest a TE. However, the cited
portion of the minutes by RPR, Covenant Presbytery minutes
159-11.8, begins “The motion was seconded and unanimously
passed (emphasis added), to divest [named TE] from the
office of TE in accordance with BCO 34-10.” A unanimous
vote surpasses a 2/3 threshold and was rightly noted in the
minutes.

description: No record that upon an attempt of a PCA TE to transfer to the PC(USA) that Presbytery judged whether or not the PC(USA) is failing to maintain the Word and Sacraments in their fundamental integrity.
responses: Covenant Presbytery respectfully
disagrees that it erred in this citation. BCO 38-3.a and BCO
38.3.b are taken in concert with one another. BCO 38.3.a says,
in part,”if at the time of the attempt to withdraw he is in good
standing, the irregularity shall be recorded, and his new
membership acknowledged.” BCO 38.3.b says in part, “when
a member or minister of the Presbyterian Church in America
shall attempt to withdraw from the communion of this branch
of the visible Church by affiliating with a body judged by the
court of original jurisdiction as failing to maintain the Word
and Sacraments in their fundamental integrity…”. The
acknowledgement of the irregularity (noted in the minutes as
“dismissed to” [note not “transferred”]” and his new
membership recorded in the minutes fulfills the requirement
of BCO 38-3.a. Committee members may disagree with the
conclusion of a presbytery on a certain matter, but that is not
a reason fora citation of substance.
A determination must be made whether
the new ecclesiastical body is a branch of the visible church,
permitting irregular removal ( BCO 38-3.a.), or whether the
new ecclesiastical body is a body failing to maintain the Word
and Sacraments in their fundamental integrity in order to warn
the TE of the danger of such attempted affiliation ( BCO 383.b.).

description: A complaint may not be dismissed, and the Presbytery “shall hear the complaint,” unless there is evidence that the complainant currently does not have standing or has withdrawn or abandoned his complaint.
responses: Covenant Presbytery respectfully
disagrees that it erred in this citation. RPR notes “A complaint
may not be dismissed, and the Presbytery “shall hear the
complaint,” unless there is evidence that the complainant
currently does not have standing or has withdrawn or
abandoned his complaint.” The citation references Covenant
Presbytery minutes 158-11.2. Covenant Presbytery minutes
158-11.2 explicitly state that the Complainant “no longer has
standing to prosecute this Complaint” and notes that the
Complainant and family had joined another church outside of
the PCA. RPR may take issue with the language of
“dismissed”, but that would be notation of form not substance.
No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( RAO 16-10.b.1). The complainant was still a member
of the PCA with standing to prosecute a complaint when the
complaint was dismissed without a hearing, and Presbytery
has not rescinded the unconstitutional denial of the complaint.
Once the unconstitutional action is rescinded,
acknowledgement of judicial mootness would be appropriate.

description: Extraordinary clause “invoked” to waive six-month membership of a candidate without a vote by presbytery.
responses: Presbytery is unsure how to respond to the
exception. The wording appears to be identical to exception
2024-4, which references page 7 of the records. However,
2024-5 references page 17 of the records, and page 17 does
not mention a candidate coming under care, or Presbytery
invoking an extraordinary clause.

description: Potential candidate not a member of PCA church; presbytery voted to “waive” requirement, but BCO 18 makes no such provision for waiving this requirement.
responses: We agree with the exception. Our
intention was to invoke the extraordinary circumstance
permitted in BCO 18-2: an applicant for care shall be a
member of the congregation for at least six-months ... “except
in those cases deemed extraordinary by the Presbytery.” The
Presbytery judged this to be an extraordinary case for the
following reasons: 1) Mr. [Name Omitted] is pastoring his
own church full time, making it difficult to attend or become
a member of a PCA church, 2) Mr. [Name Omitted] had been
undergoing supervision and training by the pastors of [Church
Name Omitted]?}, and 3) Mr. [Name Omitted] desires to
bring his church into the PCA. We will correct the minutes as
follows: “MSC to waive the 6-month membership
requirement by invoking the extraordinary clause of BCO 182 for the following reasons: 1) Mr. [Name Omitted] is
pastoring his own church full time, making it difficult to
attend or become a member of a PCA church, 2) Mr. [Name
Omitted] had been undergoing supervision and training by the
pastors of [Church Name Omitted], and 3) Mr. [Name
Omitted] desires to bring his church into the PCA.”
The situation presented still does not
resolve the issue that there is no provision in BCO 18 for
approving a candidate that is not a member of the PCA (i.e.,
“the Church” in BCO 18-1). The “extraordinary clause” is not
applicable to cases of membership in the Church in full
communion.
Presbytery agrees with the exception and
resolves to correct its action by removing Mr. [Name Omitted]
from the roll of candidates under care because Mr. [Name
Omitted] is not a member of any church in the PCA.
No record that Presbytery corrected its
action or record ( RAO 16-10.b.1) Presbytery has not recorded
removal of candidate from the roll of candidates.

Ministry at [University Name Omitted] because a
professorship of ministry at [University Name Omitted] is a
valid call to gospel ministry, considering that he will be
equipping and teaching students for ministry in the church
Presbytery's response fulfills the

valid call. However, Presbytery's response fails to fulfill the

responses: Presbytery respectfully disagrees with the

ordination requirements of BCO 21-4.c(2), and (3), except in
the limited use of the extraordinary clause ( BCO 21-4.i.), by
3/4 majority vote of Presbytery. Presbytery has not recorded

description: Process of BCO 13-2 and 34-10 not followed in cases of TEs without call for three or more years.
responses: Presbytery disagrees with this exception.
In order to comply with BCO 13-2 and 34-10, Presbytery
adopted the following Standing Rule: 2.8. The Stated Clerk
shall report at each Stated Meeting the names of any Teaching
Elders who have three consecutive un-excused absences from
Stated Meetings and any Teaching Elders who have been
without call to a particular work for 3 years (BCO13-2). 2.8.1
The Stated Clerk shall recommend the action, if any,
Presbytery should take. This Report was made for each of the
three (3) Stated Meetings in 2023 [p. 54, 95, & 135], noting
that all Teaching Elders, who are without call, regardless of
whether the three (3) year period has passed, are currently
filing our pulpits on a regular basis and meeting the needs of
our small congregations. Of our 39 churches, 17 do not have
full-time pastors. There is only 1 Teaching Elder who is past
the three (3) period, and he is filling one of these pulpits
almost every Lord’s Day. Accordingly, no recommendation
was made to proceed under BCO 34-10 and there were no
motions from the floor to take any action.
BCO 13-2 states that BCO 34-10 shall be
followed for a TE retained on a presbytery’s rolls for more
than three years without call. Furthermore, BCO 34-10 states
that the clerk shall deliver written notice to a TE without call
for more than three years that Presbytery will consider his

description: Presbytery granted a doctrinal exception that appears to be out of accord with the fundamentals of our doctrinal standards. The examinee’s stated different was: "“2. WLC Q. 109. What are the sins forbidden in the second commandment? A. The sins forbidden in the second commandment are, all devising, counselling, commanding, using, and anywise approving, any religious worship not instituted by God himself; tolerating a false religion; the making any representation of God, of all or of any of the three persons, either inwardly in our mind, or outwardly in any kind of image or likeness of any creature whatsoever... My exception here is in regards to the phrase “of any of the three persons...” With reference to Jesus Christ, no image should be made depicting His divinity. However He has appeared in the flesh, presenting to us His image. To image the humanity of Christ I do not believe is forbidden in the second commandment. Thus by application, I have no problem with the making of films depicting the life of Christ. God has imaged Himself in the Son. Secondly, the phrase “or outwardly any kind of image or likeness of any creature whatsoever...” I take to be specifically prohibited for the purpose of worship. To disconnect it from that qualifier makes the commandment itself reduce to absurdity because it would prohibit all paintings, art, photography or anything else that represents any creature of God’s creation.” As it stands, this statement seems to say we can make “images” that picture the incarnate second person of the Trinity as opposed to merely representing his humanity. If that is the position it would seem to strike at the vitals of religion.
responses: RPR's objection to the stated exception is
unclear. We discern RPR is troubled by TE [Name Omitted]'s
statement that "no image should be made depicting [Jesus's]
divinity ... [but] [t]o image the humanity of Christ I do not
believe is forbidden in the second commandment … [because]
God has imaged himself in the Son." Upon review and further
consideration, Heartland Presbytery is convinced that this
articulation of an exception to WLC 109 does in fact strike at
the vitals of religion. If this was RPR's intended objection, we
agree with it. To articulate the exception in this way without
further clarification would deny the hypostatic union of the
two natures of Jesus Christ (fully God and fully man) united
inseparably in the one true person of Jesus, the incarnate Son
of God and second person of the Trinity.
For a stated exception to WLC 109 not to strike at the
vitals of religion it must acknowledge that imaging the second
person of the Trinity can only imperfectly and incompletely
reflect the two natures of Jesus united in his one person. And
such an image can never purport to reflect only one of those
natures (fully man) lest it become a false Christ and fall into
the heresy of Nestorianism and a denial of the hypostatic
union.
In order to provide clarity on this issue we required
TE [Name Omitted] to engage with RPR’s concern, as best as
we understand it, and appear before Presbytery to state his
difference to WLC 109, if any, as he currently holds it. His
stated different is:
First, let me clearly state that I firmly believe, contra
both Nestorianism and Eutychianism, the historic orthodox
view that Christ is one person in two natures (since the
incarnation), and remains so forever (inseparably and
indivisibly united). I agree with Calvin who states, “…we
affirm his divinity so joined and united with his humanity that
each retains its distinctive nature unimpaired, and yet these
two natures constitute one Christ.” [Calvin, Institutes,
2.14.1.]. Thus, with Calvin I affirm that though there is no
division in the unity of the person of Christ in regards to His
person, there is “distinction.” (For example, consider the
historic teaching of the “communicatio idiomatum”
(communication of properties).
Second, I wish to acknowledge that my statements
made in my transfer exam could have been clearer. i in noway
believe it is possible to make a representation of Christ that
truly images the fullness of the person of Christ. Similarly, I
in no way mean imply that we can or should ever make images
to somehow even capture the full human nature of Christ.
What I meant to refer to in my original comment on my
exception is the human physical body of Christ. Thus, I wrote,
“My exception here is in regards to the phrase ‘of any of the
three persons...’ With reference to Jesus Christ, no image
should be made depicting His divinity. However, He has
appeared in the flesh, presenting to us His image. To image
the humanity of Christ I do not believe is forbidden in the
second commandment.” (emphasis added). My last sentence
is unclear as I use both the terms “flesh” and “humanity” in a
way that may be understood to equate them. I should have
said, “He has appeared in the flesh, presenting to us His
image. To image Christ in the flesh I do not believe is
forbidden in the second commandment.”
i unpack it a bit more hereby way of analogy. Though
it is an imperfect analogy (since there is no perfect analogy of
the hypostatic union), all humans are comprised of body and
soul, and no picture of a person (drawing, photograph, stick
figure, etc.) can possibly capture their human nature; it at best
can capture imperfectly that which is visible (i.e. their
materially visible “tent”). Similarly, we cannot and should not
make images of the human or divine natures of Christ.
However, I do not believe that it is sinful to make or imagine
in our minds images of Christ dwelling in the flesh on earth
during his earthly ministry. I do not believe this position in
any way destroys or divides the unity of the one Person of
Christ in two natures.
Third, WLC 109 forbids, “making any representation
of God, of all or of any of the three persons, either inwardly
in our mind, or outwardly in any kind of image or likeness of
any creature whatsoever…” The key concept that I take
exception to here is the prohibition to making any kind of
image “inwardly in our mind.” Consider our reading any of
the many descriptions of Christ in the gospels. How can we
not picture those events in our minds? How do we not picture
the hand of Christ scooping up clay to make salve for the blind
man’s eyes? How do we not picture the nails and sword
piercing Christ’s hands and side? How do we not picture
Christ walking with the men on the road to Emmaus or on the
water with Peter? On the contrary, I believe we are meant to
picture those events (as deficient as our images may be).
Therefore, though we are not to make images of God,
for teaching purposes I believe it is not sinful to make images
of Jesus’ physical form (flesh). i do not believe that to do so
somehow divides the indivisible union of the two natures in
Christ in one person.
Heartland Presbytery finds this stated difference to be
more than semantic, but not out of accord with any
fundamental of our system of doctrine ( RAO 16-3.e.6.c).

description: No record of the names of ruling elders in attendance.
responses: As stated in response to a similar
exception last year, the JRP disagrees with this exception of
substance. While names of attending ruling elders were not
included in the minutes, the record shows on the listed pages
that churches were either considered present or absent (noted
by the "P" and the "U" next to the names of each church) by
the presence of commissioned ruling elders. BCO 13-1 makes
it clear that a Presbytery is comprised of TEs and her
churches, not the specific ruling elders. We consider our
record sufficient to satisfy BCO 13-4. The JRP has included
the sign-in sheets in the 2024 minutes for the 2024 stated
meetings in response to this exception.
RAO 16-3.c.6 Requires that “The names
of those present at the meeting” be recorded. This information
is required for various purposes, including determining which
ruling elders have the right of complaint, and which members

description: There are no reviews of the minutes of Sessions in 2024. This is at least the second year in a row for this exception.

There are no annual reports for over half the TEs serving out of bounds (including missionaries with non-PCA calls), there are almost no reports from TEs without call, and there are no annual reports for about half the men under care.
— No record of sessional endorsements or six-months membership for ones coming under care; no record of charges to candidates; no record of the nature of internships that are established.

Presbytery ruled the following stated difference put forth by a candidate for licensure to be more than semantic, but not "out of accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine." "I'd like to request an exception to WLC 109 where it speaks against making a representation of God. I personally believe that the second commandment refers to not making images of "idols" (cf. the NLT translation) rather than not making images of any persons of the Godhead." If the candidate views the making of images to any of the persons of the Godhead as an acceptable practice, it would seem that this is "out of accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine because the difference is either hostile to the system or strikes at the vitals of religion" ( BCO 19-2.b.3.f). The candidate's position seems to run directly contrary to Deuteronomy 4:15-17, and it appears to undercut the regulative principle of worship as set forth in WCF XXI-1; WLC 109 and WSC 51.

Presbytery may not amend its Standing Rules to mandate that TE members of Presbytery pay an annual $50 fee, nor may Presbytery charge a mandatory fee for examinations. This would be tantamount to a local session mandating that all church members pay a fee to be examined for membership or to be members in good standing.

( BCO 24-1, 40-4, 11-4; BCO Preface II-6) — Minutes contain several references to men having passed ‘the examination for ruling elders.” This appears to be the presbytery exercising oversight over who may be elected as a ruling elder.
responses: Korean Central Presbytery (hereafter
“KCP”) acknowledges CRPR’s concern about the possibility
of the Presbytery exercising undue oversight over local
congregations. But we also respectfully note that the churches
in our Presbytery elect their own ruling elder candidates. KCP
examines these nominees only at the request of these church
Sessions who deem themselves unable to administer these
examinations on their own. The churches then install the RE
nominees after they successfully complete their examinations
by presbytery. While this seems to be a common practice
among some Korean-language Presbyteries in the PCA, KCP
acknowledges it is not ideal. We hope to move in a direction
where our local church Sessions can examine their own
nominees for Ruling Elders per BCO 12-5.b and BCO 24-1.
Presbytery did not respond according to
RAO 16-10.b. Presbytery can either agree with the exception
or respectfully disagree with the exception.
Presbytery agrees with the exception and
acknowledges our error. We have corrected our actions by
discontinuing our practice of administering examinations to
RE candidates.

description: ( BCO 21-4; RAO 16-3.e.5) — Stated differences not recorded in the minister’s/candidates own words.
responses: Korean Central Presbytery agrees with the
exception. At our October 2021 Stated Meeting, an ordination
candidate, [Name Omitted], noted the following stated
difference:
I have a different view of the “Sabbath” than WFC Chapter
21-8. I affirm with the Westminster statement that man needs
to “observe a holy rest” on the Sabbath or Lord’s day. This
means that man is to be separate from work, so that the
Sabbath day looks different from other days. I affirm that man
is not good at resting, there are many activities that one may
consider resting that in fact is “profaning the day by idleness”
(WLC 119). I believe that the specific definition of “holy rest”
being that one should rest from “their works, words, and
thoughts about their worldly employments and recreations”
goes too far in its prohibitions. The statement “spend the
whole time in public and private exercises of worship” fails
to allow for creational rest and refreshment that can include
such recreational activities as running, walking, and other
God-glorifying, family nurturing actives as WLC 121 affirms.
To do Sabbath worship and duties “all the day” and “the
whole time” would seem at tension with the great significance
Scripture also teaches on rest.
KCP adopted its Candidates and Credentials Committee’s
recommendation to judge this stated difference to be “more
than semantic but ‘not out of accord with any fundamental
system of our doctrine.’”
At our April 2022 Stated Meeting, a licensure candidate,
[Name Omitted], noted the following stated difference from
WCF 21-8, WLC 117 and 119, and WSC 60-61:
I have a scruple concerning the forbidding of worldly
recreations on the Lord’s day. I believe that recreation, such
as the playing of sports or games can be a lawful form of rest
on the Lord’s day.
KCP adopted its Candidates and Credentials Committee’s
recommendation to judge this stated difference to be “more
than semantic but ‘not out of accord with any fundamental
system of our doctrine.’”
We apologize for not including these stated differences in our
minutes, and we promise to be more careful in the future.
Presbytery apologized and promises to be
more careful in the future, but needs to amend the record and
indicate as such in responses ( RAO 16-10.b.1).
Presbytery agrees with the exception and
once again acknowledges our error. We will correct our
Minutes for the relevant Stated Meetings, and we promise to
be more careful in the future.

description: There is insufficient record in the minutes of actions taken by the Presbytery (as well as documents in support of those actions) pursuant to their handling of an ecclesiastical trial against TE (Name Omitted), such as, but not limited to the allegations against him, the recommendations from an ad interim committee appointed to consider these allegations, the indictment of the prosecutor, the imposition and removal of censure, and several additional motions related to this situation. These missing items need to be either recreated or collected and submitted to the 50th General Assembly.
responses: Korean Central Presbytery agrees with
this exception and humbly apologizes for our oversight in this
matter. We are submitting the following documents to the
50th General Assembly in response to this request by CRPR:
(1) the original allegations against the TE as submitted to the
Presbytery; (2) the findings and recommendations of the adinterim committee as summarized in their report as received
by the Presbytery; and (3) the minutes from this stated
meeting, which summarizes our actions on the committee’s
recommendations. In addition, some of the actions from this
April 2021 were the subject of a complaint that was
adjudicated by the Standing Judicial Commission in June
SJC Case 2021-11). While the SJC
sustained the complaint against some of Presbytery’s
procedural errors in this matter, it also ruled that many of these
defects were no longer correctable, including the absence of
an indictment from the prosecutor.
Though Presbytery acknowledges their
error and has sent some documents, the report from the
interim committee seems to be missing their respective and
referenced appendices (see: RAO 16-3.e.8), in addition to a
letter of confession from the confessing TE.
Appendices have been received;
confession letter has not been received.
Presbytery agrees with the exception and
acknowledges our error. The TE’s confession letter has been
submitted as requested.

description: TEs are received from other PCA presbyteries but no evidence of action of other Presbyteries.
responses: We apologize for the error in not
recording the action of the other Presbyteries. We have now
corrected this oversight and it is officially on record that TEs
[names omitted] were all released from their respective
Presbyteries.

description: Presbytery uses candidacy examination procedures for internship, so various steps are missing, including determination of the nature of the internship.
responses: We apologize, as the Presbytery agrees
with this exception and regrets the error. The intern in
question was indeed examined on his inward call to the
ministry of the Word. The Presbytery will be more diligent in
the future to ensure that all specific requirements of internship
examinations are properly recorded.

description: Minutes show that commissions met and who was present, but there are no reports showing what the commissions did.
responses: We apologize for this oversight and will
ensure that the minutes of commissions are properly included
in the official record. At KSWOC’s March 11, 2025 meeting,
the minutes from March 14, 2023, were corrected by
appending the commission minutes through a motion to
“Amend Something Previously Adopted.”

description: Presbytery does not follow BCO 38-3 for men who have joined other denominations.
responses: We apologize for this oversight. The men
who transferred were in good standing, and their irregularity
was amended. At KSWOC’s March 11, 2025 meeting, this
issue was addressed and corrected.

description: No record of annual reports of TEs laboring out of bounds.
responses: We apologize for our lack of diligence in
receiving the annual reports from TEs laboring out of bounds.
Presbytery has instructed the Stated Clerk to send a
communication to all TEs laboring out of bounds, requesting
that they submit their annual reports.

description: Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes, and these must be presented for review next year.
responses: We apologize for this oversight and will
ensure that commission minutes are properly recorded
moving forward. At KSWOC’s March 11, 2025, meeting, the
minutes from March 14, 2023, were corrected by appending
the commission minutes through a motion to “Amend
Something Previously Adopted.”

description: No record that Presbytery made determinations required by BCO 38-3.
responses: We apologize for the oversight. The
Presbytery commits to being more careful in the future. The
Presbytery has voted to correct the minutes from March 14,
2023, by noting the irregularity under 38-3.a.

description: Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes, and these must be presented for review next year.
responses: We apologize for this oversight and will
ensure that commission minutes are properly recorded in the
future. At KSWOC’s March 11, 2025, meeting, the March 14,
2023, minutes were corrected by appending the commission
minutes through a motion to “Amend Something Previously
Adopted.”

description: No record that the Presbytery is assured that an out-of-bounds TE will have full freedom to maintain and teach the doctrine of our Church.
responses: We apologize for the oversight. While the
terms of call for TEs laboring out of bounds were reviewed
and approved, the Presbytery failed to explicitly record the
assurance that the TEs would be engaged in preaching,
teaching the Word, and maintaining our doctrine in
accordance with BCO 8-7. The Presbytery commits to being
more diligent in documenting this in the future. At the March
11, 2025 meeting, the March 14, 2023 minutes were
corrected.

description: Complaint sent to Presbytery not recorded in minutes. (“full and accurate record” RAO 16-3.e.6.).
responses: This complaint was deemed to be a private
manner and it was not dealt with in the presbytery other than
noting the complaint. Due to the sensitive nature of this
private matter, it would be difficult to redress this issue and
include the specifics in the minutes.
The complaint and the response of the
Presbytery must be included in the record of the Presbytery
( BCO 43-3, 6) and submitted for review ( BCO 40-1).
We appreciate your diligence in
addressing the matter raised in your Exception to our Minutes
dated March 10, 2020, concerning the complaint sent to the
Presbytery not being recorded. We acknowledge your
assertion that both the complaint and the Presbytery's
response should be documented in accordance with the Book
of Church Order ( BCO ) regulations. However, we maintain
that in exceptional cases involving confidential or private
matters, discretion must be exercised to uphold the integrity
of the process and protect the individuals involved (especially
because there is no explicit requirement to print the Complaint
in the Minutes in BCO 43). Due to the sensitive matter of the
complaint, it was assigned to a Commission, which
adjudicated it until it reached the final resolution which was
recommended to depose the former teaching elder. At the
Presbytery meeting of Sept 15, 2020, the resolution was
presented in front of the presbytery to receive the Complaint
and full report of the commission and accept the committee’s
recommendations.
The complaint and the response of the
Presbytery must be included in the record of the Presbytery
( BCO 43-3, 6) and submitted for review ( BCO 40-1) and
include the minutes of the commission ( BCO 15-3). If the
complaint was dealt with in executive session by Presbytery,
it may be submitted under RAO 16-3.e.7 so that RPR will treat
it confidentially. Otherwise, the complaint must be submitted
through normal channels.
Presbytery acknowledges the exception
and regrets the oversight. The complaints and minutes of the
commission are on file, and the record has been corrected
through a motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted,
appending the complaints and judicial commission minutes.
Moving forward, Presbytery will be more diligent in ensuring
this matter is handled properly.
Presbytery needs to also submit the
complaint and minutes of the commission for review as
instructed by the 51st General Assembly.

description: A TE has begun to minister out of bounds. No record that Presbytery followed the appropriate BCO provisions to approve that.
responses: We apologize for the clerical mistake of
not recording the calls for TEs approved for out-of-bounds
ministry. TE [name omitted] received a call from [church
name omitted], and TEs requested approval for out-of-bound
ministry.
Presbytery must also note that it received
assurance that the TE laboring out of bounds will have full
freedom to maintain and teach the doctrine of our church and
that he will be engaged in preaching and teaching the Word

description: A commission must be appointed by Presbytery, not a committee.
responses: We apologize for the clerical mistake.
The correct wording is that the commission was appointed by
the presbytery, not a committee or the officers.
Presbytery must also correct its record by
remedying the clerical error.

description: No record of endorsement by candidates’ Sessions, six-months membership for candidates, nor of charge given to candidates.
responses: We apologize for the lack of
documentation regarding the endorsements. The candidates
had been endorsed by their Sessions, and those endorsements
were reviewed by the Candidates and Credentials Committee.
We sincerely apologize for this oversight.
Presbytery must also correct its record to
show that the candidates met the proper requirements.

description: No record of calls to a definite work.
responses: We apologize for our negligence in not
properly recording the call. The TE sent a call letter, which
was received and evaluated by the Credentials Committee.
The call was also reviewed and accepted by the floor. We will
ensure that such important details are accurately recorded in
the future.
Presbytery has apologized and promises
to be more careful in the future, but it must also correct its
record to show that the call was received and approved.

description: No sermon and no statement of differences with the Standards for one being licensed.
responses: We apologize for our negligence in
failing to record his own words in the minutes. We will be
more attentive and diligent in ensuring that such details are
accurately documented in the future.
Presbytery must also correct its record
and document whether the candidate preached a sermon as
required and also note the candidate’s exception(s) in his own
words, along with the Presbytery’s judgment of the stated
difference(s).

description: No evidence of educational requirements, required papers, or sermon for one being ordained; and no evidence the man was asked to state differences with the Standards.
responses: We apologize for our oversight in not
recording his own words in the minutes. We will be more
diligent and attentive in the future to ensure such details are
properly documented.
Presbytery must also correct its record
and note whether the candidate preached a sermon. Presbytery
must also document the candidate’s exception(s) in his own
words, along with the Presbytery’s judgment of the stated
difference(s).

description: Presbytery particularizes mission works with no evidence that all required procedures were followed – specifically no evidence of congregational meeting or petition.
responses: We apologize for the failure to properly
record the steps outlined in BCO 5-9 for organizing a
particular church. The steps, including receiving the petition
( BCO 5-9.g), appointing an organizing commission ( BCO 59.h), and setting the date and time for the organizing service
( BCO 5-9.h), were all completed, but unfortunately, they were
not documented correctly. We will ensure that such details are
accurately recorded in the future.
Presbytery must also correct its record to
show that the required steps in BCO 5-9 were in fact
completed.

description: TEs are reported as resigning from or having left current calls from churches of Presbytery and/or are recorded as taking new calls, but no evidence that Presbytery dissolved pastoral relations, nor acted on the new calls, nor is there evidence that 8-7 and 21-1 were followed for out of bounds calls.
responses: We apologize for the failure to properly
record the steps outlined. The Presbytery received all
documentations pertaining to the dissolve of the pastoral
relations and the new call.
Presbytery must also correct its record to
show that it received all of the required documentation for
dissolution of pastoral relations, and followed the steps for
approving out-of-bounds calls as specified in BCO 8-7.

description: The minutes record that a man has transferred to another Presbytery. No record that congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations, that Presbytery dissolved the pastoral relation, nor that Presbytery voted to release the TE to a different Presbytery.
responses: We apologize for failing to properly
record the concurrences of the Sessions regarding the
dissolution of their respective pastoral relations. Although the
Sessions of all pastors had concurred with these decisions, the
Presbytery neglected to document them accurately including
the Presbytery’s vote to release the TE to another presbytery.
Moving forward, we will be more diligent in ensuring such
matters are properly recorded.
Presbytery must also correct its record to
show that it received the congregations’ concurrence for
dissolving the pastoral relations, that the Presbytery dissolved
the relationships, and that it approved the TEs transfers to
different Presbyteries.

description: Stated difference not recorded in the candidate’s own words.
responses: We apologize for the oversight in not
recording his words in the minutes. We will be more diligent
and attentive in the future to ensure such details are accurately
documented.
Presbytery must also correct its record
and record the candidate’s exception(s) in his own words,
along with the Presbytery’s judgment of the stated
difference(s).

description: Stated differences not judged with the prescribed categories.
responses: We apologize for failing to capture his
words in the minutes. Moving forward, we will make sure to
carefully document all relevant details to prevent such
oversights.
Presbytery must also correct its record
and show how it judged the candidate’s stated differences
according to the prescribed categories ( RAO 16-3.e.6).

description: no record of call to a definite work.
responses: We apologize for the oversight in not
properly recording the call. The TE sent a call letter, which
was received and evaluated by the Credentials Committee.
The call was then presented to the floor, where it was
acknowledged and accepted.
Presbytery must also correct its record
and show that it received and approved the call.

description: Incomplete record of ordination exam. Men are approved for ordination but no evidence of education requirements, required papers, or original languages.
responses: We apologize for the oversight in not
properly documenting the candidate’s examination in all
required areas. The candidate was examined in committee on
all aspects, including the items mentioned above, and was
subject to open examination. At KSWOC’s March 11, 2025
meeting, the March 14, 2023 minutes were corrected by
appending the commission minutes through a motion to
“Amend Something Previously Adopted.”
The response indicates a correction to the
record for a different meeting (March 2023) than the one that
was originally cited for the exception (September 2022).

description: No record of written credentials of dismissing Presbytery (not specified) for TE transfer.
responses: We apologize for the oversight in not
properly documenting the written credentials of the
dismissing Presbytery. We can assure you all documents were
properly received.
Presbytery must also correct its record
and note that the written credentials of the dismissing
Presbytery were received.

description: All specific requirements of three licensure exams not recorded. Further, those seeking licensure are not required to be examined in sacraments and church history.
responses: We apologize for the oversight in not
properly documenting the candidate’s examination. The
candidate was thoroughly examined in committee on all areas,
including the items mentioned above, and was subject to
examination. At KSWOC’s March 11, 2025 meeting, the
March 14, 2023 minutes were corrected by appending the
commission minutes through a motion to “Amend Something
Previously Adopted.”
Presbytery should correct its record to
show that the candidate was examined in all of the required
areas. Also, part of the response indicates a correction of the
record for a different meeting (March 2023) than the one that
was originally cited for the exception (September 2022).

description: The minutes record that 2 TEs have been removed from the roll because they joined another PCA Presbytery. No record that congregations concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations, that Presbytery dissolved the pastoral relations, nor that Presbytery voted to release the TEs to the different Presbyteries.
responses: We apologize for not clearly stating in the
minutes that the Stated Clerk had received the necessary
confirmation of approval from the other presbyteries
regarding the release transfers of TEs [names omitted]. Going
forward, we will ensure that the Stated Clerk’s report includes
the confirmation of appropriate actions taken by other
presbyteries, and that this is properly documented in future
minutes.
Presbytery should also correct its record
to show that they received concurrence from the
congregations of the TEs resignations, that Presbytery
dissolved the pastoral relations, and that it approved their
transfer to different Presbyteries.

description: Presbytery appoints an interim pastor with no evidence of congregational request or concurrence.
responses: We apologize for the lack of information
in the minutes. The order was that the congregation requested
an interim pastor, and the presbytery properly responded. We
will ensure that this process is more clearly documented in the
future.
Presbytery must also correct its record by
noting that it received the request from the church for the
stated supply relationship. Furthermore, the request should be
made specifically by the church’s Session per BCO 22-6.

description: A member of the court in good standing cannot be prevented from serving on a presbytery committee.
responses: We apologize for this clerical mistake. TE
[name omitted] has been a member in good standing and was
not prevented from serving on our committee.
Presbytery must also correct its actions
by amending its Standing Rules to bring them into conformity
with the BCO .

description: Stated differences not judged with the prescribed categories.
responses: We apologize for our oversight in not
recording his exact words in the minutes. Moving forward, we
will be more diligent in ensuring that such records are
accurately documented.
Presbytery must also correct it record and
record how it judged the candidate’s stated differences ( RAO

description: No record that Congregation or Session concurred with the dissolution of pastoral relations.
responses: We apologize for this clerical oversight.
The Sessions of all pastors concurred in the dissolution of
their respective pastoral relations; however, the Presbytery
erred in not properly recording those concurrences. We will
take greater care in the future to ensure such matters are
accurately documented.
Presbytery should also correct its record
by noting that it received concurrence from the Sessions for
the dissolution of the pastoral relationship.

description: Stated differences not recorded in the candidate’s own words.
responses: We apologize for our negligence in failing
to record his own words in the minutes. We will be more
attentive and ensure such records are accurately documented
in the future.
Presbytery must also correct its record
and show how it judged the candidate’s stated differences

description: Presbytery allowed officers to appoint members of a commission.
responses: We apologize for the clerical mistake. The
correct wording is that the presbytery appointed the officers
as members of the commission.
Presbytery must also correct its record by
remedying the clerical error.

description: No record of endorsement by candidate’s Session.
responses: We apologize for this clerical mistake.
Our candidates had been endorsed by their Sessions, and those
endorsements were reviewed by the Credentials Committee.
Presbytery must also correct its record
and note that it received endorsement for the candidates from
their Sessions.

description: The presbytery, and not its appointees, "shall elect one of its commissioners to each of these committees of commissioners." .
The presbytery, and not its appointees, shall nominate elders to serve on Permanent Committees.
No record that Session was cited to appear before Presbytery.
No record in minutes of exceptions taken by GA.
No record that Congregation /Session concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
No record of calls from the session/Congregation for Pastoral relations.
Specific arrangements of call not shown to be approved.
No record that licentiate was asked about differences to standards.
No response to the Assembly concerning disposition of exceptions of substance.
No record in minutes of repoonses to exceptions taken by GA.
The presbytery appointed two men to do the work of a commission.
There was not an annual report from TEs laboring out of bounds or without a call.
No record of the reasons why Presbytery considers an out-of-bounds work to be a valid Christian ministry.

The minutes state that the TE gave notice for reasons for divestiture at a previous meeting, but there is no record in those minutes of such notice. The presbytery divested him without two stated meetings.

Presbytery granted a doctrinal exception that needs further clarity for proper review. While exceptions on the application of the 4th Commandment are common (e.g. recreation clause), it is quite another thing to say that the Sabbath has been fulfilled and is to be celebrated everyday instead of weekly. This would seem to be out of accord with our system of doctrine in that it appears to radically redefine one of the Ten Commandments in a way that is contrary to our confessional standards and God’s creation ordinance.
responses: The transfer candidate was approached for
clarity. He submitted the following clarification, and this was
recorded in the Oct 2, 2023 minutes: “My position is that of
John Calvin as he stated in (1) Institutes 2:8:30-34; (2) Geneva
Catechism Questions 166-184; and (3) Calvin's Commentary
Colossians 2:16-17.”
Candidates response is neither in his own
words (Rao 16-3.e.5), and does not address the issue raised by
the 50th Assembly. Candidates response must be in his own
words, and must address the apparent abrogation of the 4th
commandment implied by the Sabbath being fulfilled, and it
being celebrated everyday instead of on the Lord’s Day.
Upon further reflection, discussion and
clarification, the TE withdrew his position and has no
exception to the WCF in this area. He stated, “Upon further
reflection on the theological issues involved and having
discussed the matter with our presbytery candidates and
credentials committee, I have decided to withdraw my
exception regarding the Sabbath, and I affirm my support for
what the Westminster Standards say on this subject.”

description: No record of review of records of church Sessions.
responses: Thank you. The records were reviewed,
but we failed to record the presbytery as a whole approving.
Each meeting the RSR chair did report from the floor of the
Presbytery on church’s records. Our RSR committee has done
an excellent job of receiving and reviewing. We began
correctly noting in September 2024.

description: No provision made for installation of candidate.
responses: TE [Name Omitted] installation had not
happened. Installation is set for 3/4/25.

description: No record that congregation elected existing assistant pastor as an associate pastor on recommendation of the session.
responses: We failed to record. He was elected and
installed. [Name Omitted] (TE), [Name Omitted] (TE),
[Name Omitted] (RE) and [Name Omitted] (RE). Were the
commission. Installed Oct 15

description: No record that session requested stated supply.
responses: In an email dated 9/28/23 the session did
request. We failed to record. The letter read in part “This past
Sunday, the congregation of COTR, in a duly called
congregational meeting, reconstituted our previous pastor
search. Pursuant to BCO 22-5 & 22-6, the session has a
pdeveloped a plan for Stated Supply to meet the pastoral needs
of our congregation. The men are no longer stated supply.We
will be more circumspect in the future.

description: No record that the Congregation(/Session) concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
responses: We failed to record that the
congregation/session concurred. The communications /
correspondence was received from each congregation
regarding each of these men and their respective change of
call.

description: Stated supply approved without licensure.
responses: TE [Name Omitted] was stated supply.
This is similar to 43-2024-6. We had corespondence with the
church in 2022.

description: Censure of indefinite suspension from office administered without full record of case without process.
responses: The full record of the case, without
process, was made available. Forgive the recording err.

description: Stated supply approved without licensure.
responses: TE [Name Omitted] was one of several
who supplied at COTR. He is no longer supplying the pulpit.
We did have corespondence from the church. We will be more
circumspect in future.
The nature of the exception is not
whether or not pulpit supply was given with correspondence
from the church but whether or not the Supply in question
ought to have been approved. ( BCO 19-1) Presbytery records
still make no record of a TE [Name Omitted]; no information
can be found in directory; it is unclear who he is.

description: Presbytery granted a doctrinal exception that appears to be out of accord with the fundamentals of our doctrinal standards. It is not clear from the difference as stated if the candidate believes there are any forms of recreation that are proscribed by the commandment.
responses: In further conversation with the candidate,
his view is that the day is for worship, rest and reflection.
Labor/work, except in the case of necessity, are prohibited.
The response does not address the
question about whether there are any forms of recreation that
are proscribed by the commandment.

description: There is insufficient record in the minutes of actions taken by the Presbytery pursuant to their handling of a report concerning the Christian Character of a Teaching Elder subject to their care. Presbytery needs to record in their minutes how they handled the report against the Christian Character. .
Pastoral call dissolved without record that the congregation was cited to appear to show cause.
Two pastoral calls dissolved without record that the congregations were cited to appear to show cause.

Presbytery ruled that the TE’s view that it is permissible for a woman to preach is “not out of accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine because the difference is neither hostile to the system nor strikes at the vitals of religion.” This was error as such a view is clearly contrary to scripture

( WCF 21-5; WLC 158; BCO 4-4; 8-5; 40-5; 58-4) — Presbytery delinquent to redress a Session who admitted to unconstitutional proceedings of: (1) permitting a woman to expound the Scriptures during a worship service on the Lord’s Day; (2) holding many worship services without preaching; and (3) serving the Lord’s Supper at many services without a preceding sermon.
responses: The matters referenced in this exception
were referred to the SJC by the 50th General Assembly per
This matter is connected to, and is
answered in reference to, item (g), below.
M/S/C... The matters referenced in this
exception were referred to the SJC by the 51st General
Assembly per BCO 40-5

description: Ordination candidate’s stated differences not recorded.
Sufficient reason for withdrawal of candidate not given.
Reasons for rescission of intern status not recorded.
No record commissioners appointed by the church presented and prosecuted the calls before the Presbytery.
Incomplete record of examination of TE transferring into Presbytery.
Incomplete record of examination of TE transferring into Presbytery.
Incomplete record of ordination exam.
No record of 6-month church membership or 1-month prior filing of application for men coming under care.
responses: The presbytery thanks CRPR for their
efforts, PMV has all necessary session endorsements for those
coming under care and have improved our practice to make
sure those documents are noted in the minutes

description: No record of annual reports for the majority of men serving out of bounds or without call.
responses: PMV acknowledges this error, the annual
reports were recieved and approved, a clerical error caused the
reports to be left out or the Nov Minutes. PMV will endeavor
to be more diligent in the future in regards to annual reports.

description: No record that Presbytery followed BCO 21-4.e (as amended at the 50th GA) in conducting an ordination examination.
responses: PMV acknowledges a lack of reporting in
this area, while the men were examined according to BCO 214.e it was not recorded. PMV has corrected its recording
practices to correct his oversight

description: Committee empowered to act as a commission makes decisions via e-mail which does not constitute a deliberative assembly.
responses: PMV disagree with this citation in that,
the committee was empowering a commission but was instead
approving the makeup of a commission that would be
approved at the Aug 1 Presbytery meeting. The wording in the
minutes confusing and PMV will endeavor to clarify such
matters in the future

description: Installation commissisons' formation and composition was not approved by Presbytery.
No record of annual report of TE’s laboring out of bounds.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfully
disagrees with the exception and refers it back to the
Assembly. BCO 8-7 does not specify that the annual report of
TEs laboring out of bounds be recorded in Presbytery
Minutes. The BCO specifies information to be included in
Presbytery minutes in a number of places; therefore, the
absence of language requiring that reports from TEs laboring
out of bounds be recorded in the minutes must be viewed as
intentional. Although not required to do so, Missouri
Presbytery will include a record of the annual reports received
from TEs laboring out of bounds. Each such report is
forwarded to the Presbytery upon its receipt.

description: No record of change of status from licensed to senior pastor.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfully
disagrees with the exception and refers it back to the
Assembly. The referenced Licentiate was admitted to the
Presbytery as a Senior Pastor at the January 20, 2024 stated
meeting of Missouri Presbytery. In preparing the report to the
Review of Presbytery Records, the Directory was compiled
on February 6, 2024 and is accurate as of that date.

description: No record that presbytery is assured that out of bounds TE has full freedom to maintain/teach doctrine of PCA.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfully
disagrees with the exception and refers it back to the
Assembly. Two calls are specifically referenced. In the first
case (TE [Name Omitted]), the terms of the call, attached to
the minutes, provide express assurance of his freedom to
maintain and teach the doctrine of the PCA. In the second case
(TE [Name Omitted]), the call is issued by Mission to North
America, a PCA permanent committee. Because that call is
not from an "organization outside the jurisdiction of the
Presbyterian Church in America" the BCO 8-7 requirement
that the presbytery be assured of "full freedom to maintain and
teach the doctrine of our Church" does not apply.

description: No record that Congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
responses: Missouri Presbytery agrees with the
exception, has corrected the record by including a notation in
Resolution 2301-46 that the Congregation concurred with the
dissolution of the pastoral relation without objection (page
2554 of the 2024 minutes), and promises to be more careful
in the future.

description: 3/4 vote for waiving internship requirements not recorded.
responses: Missouri Presbytery agrees with the
exception, has corrected the record by including a notation
that Resolution 2301-11 passed without objection (page 2553
of the 2024 minutes), and promises to be more careful in the
future.

description: No record of vote from congregation calling pastor or record of 4/5 majority required.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfuly disagrees
with the exception as presented and refers it back to the
Assembly. Missouri Presbytery did not meet on October 7,
2023 but assumes that the exception refers to its stated
meeting on October 17, 2023. None of the cited provisions of
the BCO refer to a requirement to record the vote of the
congregation nor of a required 4/5 majority vote. The
Presbytery received a report of the unanimous vote of the
congregation as a part of the pastor's call document. Pursuant
to RAO 16-3.e.7, the call document is not required to be
attached to the minutes, but the minutes are required to record
that the specific arrangements of the call were found to be in
order (which would include the congregational vote and, if
applicable, a requirement fora specific majority vote). That
determination was duly recorded in Resolution 2310-16. A
clarifying amendment to that Resolution was adopted to note
that the call was approved by the congregation without
objection.

description: Allegations against a pastor(s) and session received by presbytery not recorded in the presbytery minutes.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfully
disagrees with the exception and refers it back to the
Assembly. None of the cited authorities require the recording
of allegations that do not lead to either a BCO 32-1 or BCO
40-5 investigation. The BCO and RAO specify, with great
detail, information to be included in Presbytery minutes in a
number of places; therefore, the absence of language requiring
that such allegations be recorded in the minutes must be
viewed as intentional. Further, the church against which the
allegations were made voted to withdraw from the
denomination, pursuant to BCO 25-11, on December 3, 2023,
which withdrawal was presented to Missouri Presbytery at its
January 20, 2024 stated meeting and the church's TEs were
released by Missouri Presbytery at that same stated meeting.

description: 3/4 vote for extraordinary clause not recorded.
responses: Missouri Presbytery agrees with the
exception, has corrected the record by including a notation
that Resolution 2301-27 passed without objection (page 2553
of the 2024 minutes), and promises to be more careful in the
future.

description: No record that TE released from call and transferring to a different denomination was removed from the rolls.
responses: Missouri Presbytery can neither agree nor
disagree with the exception without further explanation of
what record is expected or required. The referenced TE was
released to another denomination pursuant to Resolution
2301-26 and was removed from the Directory.

description: Annual report of interns not included.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfully
disagrees with the exception of substance and refers it back to
the Assembly. BCO 19-12 states: "Reports on every intern in
the Presbytery should be presented at each stated meeting of
the Presbytery by the committee of Presbytery charged with
the oversight of interns, and these reports shall become a part
of the minutes of Presbytery. The Presbytery shall also require
every intern himself to make a report to it at least once a year
describing his ministerial experiences. If the intern is still in
school, the Presbytery shall secure from his instructors an
annual report upon his deportment, diligence, and progress in
study." This paragraph address three types of reports - (1)
Reports on every intern; (2) Reports from every intern; and
(3) Reports from instructors of those interns still in school.
Missouri Presbytery assumes that the language of the
exception ("report of interns") refers to the reports from every
intern. The BCO is very clear that the only reports that are
required to be included in the minutes of Presbytery are the
reports on the interns. Furthermore such reports "on" interns
are not themselves mandatory as the BCO states that such
reports "should" be made. Missouri Presbytery's Candidates
and Interns Committee presents a verbal report on the interns
at each stated meeting and the report on the interns is recorded
in the Presbytery's minutes. The BCO does not specify that the
reports submitted by the interns are to be recorded in the
minutes. The BCO specifies information to be included in
Presbytery minutes in a number of places; therefore, the
absence of language requiring that reports from the interns be
recorded in the minutes must be viewed as intentional.
The fact that reports from interns were
received must be recorded in the minutes, and reports on
interns must be received at each meeting.

description: Incomplete record of review of records of church sessions.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfully
disagrees with the exception and refers it back to the
Assembly. Although not all sessions submitted minutes for
review, Missouri Presbytery reported on all session minutes
that were reviewed during 2023. Therefore there was a
complete record of the session minutes that were reviewed.
The Presbytery continues to press for compliance with this
review obligation and, further, has reviwed the manner in
which it reports the review of session records for additional
clarity and to further encourage all session to timely submit
session minutes for review.
BCO 40-1 makes it clear that the
Presbytery has a duty to review the records of all sessions
within their bounds. A review is incomplete even if the
presbytery reviews all the records that are submitted, but fails
to receive records from all churches.

description: No record reflecting when a man who was transferred to another presbytery was received by that presbytery.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfully
disagrees with the exception and refers it back to the
Assembly. BCO 13-7 refers to ordination and to ministers
admitted into membership in the Presbytery, not to those
transferring to another Presbytery. BCO 46-6 does not require
that a record of reception by another presbytery to which ate
is transferred be recorded in the minutes. A record of
reception of a transferred TE is maintained in the Presbytery
Portal maintained by the Office of the Stated Clerk for such
purposes.
The Presbytery Portal is not equivalent to
the Presbytery’s minutes. The Portal can serve as a “certificate
of dismission” but the reception of the TE needs to be
recorded in both Presbyteries’ minutes once it occurs.

description: No recording regarding the reception of a certificate of dismissal from transferring presbytery.
responses: Missouri Presbytery respectfully
disagrees with the exception and refers it back to the
Assembly. BCO 46-6 does not require that a record of
dismissal by another Presbytery from which a TE is
transfering into Missouri Presbytery be recorded in the
minutes. a record of dismissal of a TE transferring into
Missouri Presbytery is maintained in the Presbytery Portal
maintained by the Office of the Stated Clerk for such
purposes.
The Presbytery Portal is not the minutes
of the Presbytery and the record of dismission from a previous
presbytery needs to be recorded in the minutes.

description: Presbytery judged the Episcopal Church as another branch of the visible church – which faithfully maintains the Word and Sacraments – by utilizing BCO 38-3.a instead of utilizing BCO 38-3.b and thereby judge this body as “failing to maintain the Word and Sacraments in their fundamental integrity.”.

responses: We agree with the exception. We have
since amended our standing rules to create a particular
committee charged with annual review of all session minutes
and have elected the members to serve on the committee. The
committee as completed a thorough review of session records
and will submit its report for action at our 116th Stated
Meeting (November 14, 2023).
The Committee to review Session
Records reported at the November 2023 stated meeting with
a sampling of the types of exceptions the committee noted in
the minutes of Sessions from 2022, but no recommendations
were brought forth for the Presbytery to adopt. Only the
Presbytery or a commission formed for that purpose can cite
a lower court with an exception to their minutes.
We agree with the exception and respond
with reference to our response to 47-2024-2.

description: Presbytery approved a 38-2 request without a record of a “full deliberation” and not the “next stated meeting” from when the request was submitted. It also appears that Presbytery dissolved a call that had been previously dissolved (per the request letter).

acknowledge the lack of clarity in our minutes of November
12, 2023. We will seek to be more precise in the future. The
38-2 request was in fact presented to presbytery on August 8
and docketed for action at the November 12 meeting (2023
Minutes, p.122). At the November 12 meeting, presbytery
considered and granted the request as recorded in our minutes
(2023 Minutes, p. 180).
