description: Stated differences not recorded in the minister’s/candidate’s own words.
responses: A. BCO 13-6 and RAO 16-3.e.5 require
that an ordained man from another denomination coming into
the Presbytery state his differences with the Westminster
Standards, but they do not require that of men transferring
from another PCA presbytery. North Texas Minutes 2023
Page 140 records the transfer examination of TE [Name
Omitted] who was transferring from a PCA Presbytery and so
there has not been a BCO or RAO requirement of including
his stated differences. We now understand that the 51st GA
has sent down BCO Amendment Item 1 to revise BCO 13-6
with a statement that will require the recording of differences
for transfers from another PCA presbytery and this will direct
our practices in the future, assuming Item 1 is approved.
B. Never-the-less, North Texas did consider TE [Name
Omitted]’s stated differences, recording the stated differences
in his own words in the Theological Examining Committee
report materials included in the North Texas Minutes 2023
page 232.
North Texas Minutes include all the Dockets, Committee
Reports and Supplemental Items referenced in the meetings.
North Texas Minutes 2023 Page 140 states:
“MSA to receive TE [Name Omitted]’s stated differences
with the Westminster Standards as more than semantic but not
out of accord with our system of doctrine. See 152 Docket.”
The above refers to the 152 Docket which is included as part
of the Minutes. North Texas Minutes Page 232 records the
stated differences of the minister in his own words.

description: Stated differences not recorded in the minister’s/candidate’s own words.
responses: North Texas Minutes 2023 Page 411
records the examination and approval of [Name Omitted] as a
Candidate and as an Intern. BCO 18 and 19.B do not require
the statement of differences with the Westminster Standards
as part of the examinations of Candidates or Interns. RAO 163.e.5 and the BCO do require the statement of differences as
part of the licensure ( BCO 19.A), ordination ( BCO 21-4), and
incoming transfers from another denominations exams ( BCO
13-6), but these were not the examinations conducted for
[Name Omitted].

description: No record that Congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relations.
responses: The minutes as presented include record
of the full requirements for dissolving pastoral relations. A
church may be relieved of its pastor without the concurrence
of that pastor ( BCO 23-1 ¶2), and a pastor may be relieved of
his pastoral charge without the concurrence of the
congregation ( BCO 23-1 ¶1). As such dissolution of the
pastoral relations does not require mutual concurrence, it
cannot be a requirement that the presbytery demonstrate that
the mutual concurrence of both parties has been obtained. The
presbytery may show such concurrence if it chooses, but must
show (1) that either the pastor or the congregation has
requested the dissolution of the pastoral relation, and (2) that
the presbytery concurred with that request.

were not completely recorded in the minutes. We will correct
the record and be more complete in the future.

dissenting) following a called meeting of the congregation

Presbytery’s response does not acknowledge the error or
attempt to correct the record

if a complaint is properly filed, it shall be heard by the Presbytery. A complaint may not be denied without a hearing except on narrow administrative grounds.
responses: At the April 13, 2021 meeting of
Presbytery, there was an indication that the complainant
intended to withdraw his complaint and that he would not
pursue it any further nor elevate it to a higher court. The
complainant communicated such in an email to Presbytery’s
Stated Clerk on April 16, 2021. The Stated Clerk regrets the
failure to record this in the minutes.
No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( RAO 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaint.
The Northwest Georgia Presbytery agrees
with the exception. A complaint, timely filed and in order,
must be given a hearing ( BCO 43-8). The Presbytery therefore
erred in denying the complaint without a hearing. However, it
is not possible for the Presbytery to correct its error since the
complaint has been withdrawn by the complainant. This
action to withdraw the complaint was taken by the
complainant on April 16, 2021 in a letter emailed to the Stated
Clerk for circulation to the NWGP (and submitted to the
CRPR as a separate PDF attachment titled “Crouse
Withdrawal of Complainant”). There is therefore no
complaint before the Presbytery to take up or otherwise
correct. Nevertheless, the Presbytery regrets its error and
promises to adjudicate all complaints it may receive in the
future in strict adherence to the provisions of the Book of
Church Order . (The Presbytery also adopted an apology to the
complainants in the above cases and directed its Stated Clerk
to deliver the apology to them. The apology is submitted to
the CRPR as a separate PDF attachment titled “NWGP Ltr to
Complainants 1.24.24”)
No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( RAO 16-10.b.1). The Presbytery serves as an example
to others in the way it apologized to the Complainants for its
incorrect actions, but Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaint. Acknowledgement
of abandonment or withdrawal should only come after the
unconstitutional action has been rescinded because the alleged
abandonment or withdrawal occurred after the
unconstitutional dismissal by the Presbytery.
The Presbytery agrees with the exception.
The denial of the complaint has been rescinded and the
complaint ruled judicially moot. Presbytery regrets its error
and promises to adjudicate all complaints it may receive in the
future in strict adherence to the provisions of the Book of
Church Order .

description: Timely and orderly complaints (3) denied without a hearing.
responses: The Northwest Georgia Presbytery agrees
with the exception. A complaint, timely filed and in order,
must be given a hearing ( BCO 43-8). The Presbytery erred in
adopting the decision of its judicial commission in the instant
case because the judicial commission made its
recommendation without holding a hearing. However, it is not
possible for the Presbytery to correct this error since the case
(one which consolidated three complaints) has already been
appealed to and ruled upon by the SJC in two separate
decisions (SJC 2022-16 Michelson, et al. v. NWGP, & SJC
2022-05 Crouse et al. v. NWGP ). Even if the Presbytery could
rehear a case subsequently decided by the higher court, the
complaint is now moot (as the SJC ruled in both above cases)
since the underlying issue in the consolidated cases was
resolved when the Midway Session dismissed all charges
against the accused Ruling Elders and ended the judicial
process. Nevertheless, the Presbytery regrets its error and
promises to adjudicate all complaints it may receive in the
future in strict adherence to the provisions of the Book of
Church Order. (The Presbytery also adopted an apology to the
complainants in the above cases and directed its Stated Clerk
to deliver the apology to them. The apology is submitted to
the CRPR as a separate PDF attachment titled “NWGP Ltr to
Complainants 1.24.24”)
No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( RAO 16-10.b.1). The Presbytery serves as an example
to others in the way it apologized to the Complainants for its
incorrect actions, but Presbytery has not rescinded the
unconstitutional denial of the complaints. Acknowledgement
of judicial mootness should only come after the
unconstitutional action has been rescinded because the action
of the higher court occurred after the unconstitutional
dismissal by the Presbytery.
The Presbytery agrees with the exception.
The denial of the complaints has been rescinded and the
complaints ruled judicially moot. Presbytery regrets its error
and promises to adjudicate all complaints it may receive in the
future in strict adherence to the provisions of the Book of
Church Order.

Presbytery granted a doctrinal exception that appears to be hostile to the system of doctrine or strike at the vitals of religion. In the candidates own words, he says, “As regards who may partake of the Lord’s Supper, I differ with the idea that the New Testament requires covenant children to make a profession of faith (an idea not explicitly stated in the Standards) or mature to the point of self-examination prior to being admitted to the Table. I believe covenant children should be welcomed to the Table of the Lord more or less when they join the table in their homes. Three biblical arguments persuade me of this view: the OT pattern of Passover and other sacrificial feasts, the dubious universalizing of 1 Corinthians 11:28, and the unity of signification between the Sacraments.1 First, it is clear that children partook, and were commanded to partake, of the Passover meal and other sacrificial feasts in the OT (Exod 12:24-27; Deut 12:11-12; 16:10-11), which are forerunners to the Lord’s Supper much as circumcision is to baptism. The unity of the Covenant of Grace is a hallmark of Reformed thought. The Confession says that “The sacraments of the old testament, in regard of the spiritual things thereby signified and exhibited, were, for substance, the same with those of the new” ( WCF 28.5). Given these points, there ought to be a presumption of continuity as to the recipients of the sacraments of nourishment (holy feasts/Lord’s Supper) just as there is for the sacraments of initiation (circumcision/baptism). Second, the chief warrant for seeing discontinuity at this point is Paul’s injunction in 1 Corinthians 11:28: “Let a person examine himself, then, and so eat of the bread and drink of the cup.” It is argued that in this verse Paul issues a universal prerequisite for all worthy partaking in the Lord’s Supper. However, it is dubious whether this verse can be used in this way. The NT letters are occasional documents (1 Corinthians especially so), responding to concrete circumstances in first-century churches. Paul is clear in what he is addressing—exclusionary, factious, and elitist behavior toward fellow church members while ostensibly celebrating the Lord’s Supper (1 Cor 11:18-20). Schismatic behavior that tears at the unity of the body profanes the unity of the bread and the participation in the body of Christ that partaking in the bread signifies (1 Cor 10:14-17). Lots of legitimate application can be made along these lines to the Church today. But to universalize the application of this passage is to take it too far. To deduce from it that Paul intended to set a minimum maturity threshold for participation in the Supper is, in my view, to stretch it beyond the breaking point. Moreover, even if Paul’s injunction to self-examination is taken as broadly as possible, the fact remains that there is a universal injunction to repent and believe prior to baptism for adults which (contrary to Baptists) we do not require of covenant children. Third, Scripture teaches and our Standards affirm that baptism and the Lord’s Supper fundamentally agree in their significance: “the spiritual part of both is Christ and his benefits; both are seals of the same covenant” ( WLC 176). Oddly to my mind, Bavinck, and the Majority Report of the PCA on this subject with him, use this reality to assert the insignificance of withholding the Supper from young children. The children have baptism, the reasoning goes, so it’s not a big deal if they don’t get the Supper yet. But to my mind this point cuts precisely the other way. Since by baptism they already bear Christ’s name, how can they be justly denied his bread? By what token are they granted the one-time sign of union with Christ while being denied the recurring sign of union with Christ? The unity of signification between the Sacraments compels me to think that the reasons for which we baptize our covenant children hold for the question of whether or not to commune our children as well.”

description: Timely and orderly complaint dismissed without a hearing.
responses: Respectfully, the Pacific Northwest
Presbytery does not agree that this is an exception of
substance. The complaint in question was ruled out of order
by the Session, and the Presbytery similarly ruled it out of
order. The matter was subsequently carried to the Standing
Judicial Commission (SJC). Presbytery does not believe this
constitutes “an apparent violation of Scripture, a serious
irregularity from the Constitution of the PCA, an action out of
accord with a deliverance of the GA, a matter of impropriety,
an important delinquency, or non-compliance with RAO 163.e.5-6.
1. Timeliness and Order of Complaint:
RPR concluded that the complaint was “timely
and orderly” without reviewing the Record of the
Case. It is unclear how this determination could
have been made without access to the full record,
particularly when Presbytery ruled the complaint
out of order.
2. BCO 43-8 Does Not Apply:
There is no constitutional requirement for a
higher court to hold a hearing if it deems a
complaint out of order. Additionally, there is no
requirement to adopt a reason for ruling a
complaint out of order. Past SJC decisions have
demonstrated that complaints can be ruled out of
order without citing reasons. Even if the
complaint had been heard, BCO 43-9 permits a
court to deny a complaint without providing
reasons. BCO 43 does not require the higher court
to produce a decision equivalent to the standards
of the SJC Manual, §17.1.
Furthermore, BCO 43-8 specifically applies only
after the higher court has determined that a
complaint is timely and in order for it to be heard.
Since the Presbytery deemed the complaint out of
order, BCO 43-8 is not applicable in this case.
3. Judicial Matters Should Not Be Reviewed by
RPR:
According to RAO 16-4.e.9, “Minutes of
presbytery dealing with judicial cases shall not be
dealt with by Committee on Review of
Presbytery Records when notice of appeal or
complaint has been given the lower court ( BCO
40-3).” This complaint was carried to the SJC as
Case 2023-11, and a decision was rendered by the
SJC on March 7, 2024. Members of the RPR who
serve on the SJC, as well as the Pacific Northwest
Presbytery representative, could have informed
the RPR of this fact in May 2024.
For these reasons, the Pacific Northwest Presbytery
maintains that the citation is unwarranted, and further
attention to this matter is unnecessary.

description: No record that congregation concurred with dissolution of pastoral relation.
responses: We acknowledge the citation regarding
the May 19, 2023, minutes, which noted the absence of a
record that the congregation concurred with the dissolution of
Rev. [Name Omitted]’s call, as required by BCO 23-1.
Upon review, we confirm that the congregation of [Church
Name Omitted], Issaquah, formally concurred with the
dissolution of Rev. [Name Omitted]’s call in a called
congregational meeting held on May 7, 2023. The
congregation voted unanimously (48-0) in favor of the
dissolution, as recorded in the minutes of the congregational
meeting. This action was communicated to the Presbytery,
and the Ministers & Churches Committee, acting as a
commission, subsequently approved the dissolution in
accordance with Presbytery Standing Rule 4.56.e.
At its most recent meeting, the Presbytery approved an
amendment to the May 19, 2023, minutes to include this
information, ensuring compliance with BCO 23-1. The
amended minutes now reflect that the congregation formally
concurred with the dissolution, and that this concurrence was
a part of the Presbytery’s decision-making process.
We regret the original omission and are committed to
ensuring that all future minutes fully document the required
steps in similar cases.

description: Incomplete record of ordination exams.
responses: Upon review, we recognize that this
omission occurred because the May 2023 minutes referenced
Rev. [ ] as being licensed in October 2022, and noted that the

make that clear in the minutes. The minutes of the October
2022 meeting show that he was given the first part of his
ordination examination, in which he was “carefully
examined” in the areas listed in BCO 21-4.c.1.a, c, d, and h.
These areas include:
1. His Christian experience, especially personal
character and family management (21-4.c.1.a),
2. Theology (21-4.c.1.c),
3. The Sacraments (21-4.c.1.d),
4. Church government (21-4.c.1.h).
These examinations fulfilled the ordination
examination requirements in the above-listed areas. However,
theological and exegetical papers were not recorded as part of
the examination during this stage, and it is unclear as to
whether they were ever requested or received. As his
ordination is now complete, the Presbytery asks the GA to
forgive the oversight.
To address this oversight:
1. We have amended the May 19, 2023, minutes to
explicitly state the areas of examination from
October 2022 that pertain to the requirements of
2. Moving forward, we will ensure that all parts of
the ordination examination—including the
requirement for theological and exegetical
papers—are fully documented in the Presbytery
minutes as they occur.
We appreciate the Committee’s diligence in reviewing our
records and will ensure full compliance with BCO 21-4 and
RAO 16-3.e.5 going forward.

including the requirement to preach a sermon. However, these
elements were not recorded in the minutes.
To address this oversight, the minutes have been
amended to include the specific areas of examination, as
outlined in BCO 19-2: Christian experience, theology, English
Bible, church government, and the preaching of a sermon. The
relevant minutes of the Feb. 7, 2025 meeting at which we
approved this amendment are attached.
We regret the omission and appreciate the
Committee’s diligence in ensuring compliance with the BCO .
Moving forward, we will ensure that all examinations are
thoroughly documented in the minutes.

description: Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes, and these must be presented for review next year.
responses: We have received and reviewed the
citation regarding the General 2023 minutes, which noted that
the minutes of a commission were not entered into the
Presbytery minutes, as required by BCO 15-1 and RAO 163.e.4.
However, the citation does not specify which
commission is being referred to. We respectfully request that
the Committee on Review of Presbytery Records clarify
which commission and associated minutes are in question so
that we can investigate and take the appropriate corrective
action.
We remain committed to maintaining accurate and
complete records and will gladly address this matter once the
specific details are provided.
Minutes of standing commission to
approve Presbytery’s minutes not recorded.

description: No record that members of interim/provisional Session were called by congregation.
responses: We respectfully disagree with the citation
that there is “no record that members of the
interim/provisional Session were called by the congregation.”
Provisional Sessions are appointed by the Presbytery and do
not require congregational approval. Specifically:
1. BCO 5-8 assigns the authority to the Presbytery
to appoint a provisional Session for mission
churches or other churches unable to function
autonomously due to a lack of sufficient
leadership. The church at hand had one ruling
elder and no pastor.
2. BCO 12-1 requires that a Session consist of at
least one Teaching Elder and one or more Ruling
Elders, and so this church had no session. This
meant that it is the Presbytery’s responsibility to
ensure proper oversight, either by appointing a
provisional Session or through other means of
support.
3. The congregation’s role does not include calling
or approving members of a provisional Session
under the provisions of BCO 5-8 or BCO 13-1, as
these actions fall under the jurisdiction of the
Presbytery.
In this particular instance, the Presbytery
acted within its authority to address the
governance needs of the church by appointing
members to an interim/provisional Session.
While we acknowledge the importance of clearly
documenting such actions in Presbytery minutes
for the sake of clarity, the absence of
congregational approval in this process is not a
procedural error, as it is not required by the BCO .
We appreciate the Committee’s diligence in
reviewing our records and will endeavor to ensure that future
minutes explicitly document the Presbytery’s actions in
appointing provisional Sessions for clarity and completeness.
Presbytery did not address the issue
raised by Preliminary Principle 6, which indicates that a
presbytery does not have the power to elect persons to
exercise authority in any particular church. See also BCO 162.

description: Annual report of interns not included.
No record of annual reports of TEs laboring out of bounds.
Censure inflicted by a judicial commission without process being instituted or a judgment approved by the Presbytery.
responses: In the judicial case concerning T. E. [name
omitted] he consented to the recommendation made by the
Judicial Committee for definite suspension. He has pleaded
neither guilty nor not guilty to the charges made; the
Presbytery is waiting for the decision of the civil court before
it deals decisively with this case. However, Presbytery should
have made clearer in its minutes of the executive session that
“MSC” means that the motion was made, seconded, and
carried by Presbytery.
Presbytery’s response does not address
the issue. a presbytery may suspend a minister from office
following the conclusion of judicial process; or, under 31-10,
he may be suspended from his official functions while under
process. Presbytery has not indicated that the minister is under
process.
The minister in question had already
requested and been granted suspension from office by an act
of Presbytery on 2/11/2021 Minutes, Attachment E) and
remained under suspension from office, meaning that he could
not rightly exercise any of the functions of an ordained
teaching elder. The judicial process in our Presbytery had
begun with this suspension and was intensified when further
evidence surfaced of his offenses; the Presbytery deemed it
wise not to further the process until the teaching elder’s case
had been adjudicated in the civil court. The civil case took an
unusual amount of time for the adjudication to take place, and
in the end the teaching elder took an Alford plea which is
allowed in civil cases by the Supreme Court of the U.S. but
not in our church courts. At the specific request of and with
the approval of the teaching elder, the church trial was
postponed until after the civil case had been settled. The civil
case was concluded and the Presbytery in its meeting of May
11, 2023, resumed process against this teaching elder.
Arraignment was set for the August 10, 2023 meeting of
Presbytery. The teaching elder did not appear, and a motion
was made empowering the Stated Clerk to appoint a Judicial
Commission to bring the matter to a conclusion and report
back to the Presbytery. Furthermore, it was moved, seconded,
and carried in the meeting of Palmetto Presbytery on
November 9, 2023 that this action of the presbytery on
11/9/23 be added to this response to GA/RPR by March 15,
Presbytery has not adequately shown that
the TE was suspended under one of only two methods
prescribed in the BCO : (1) as a censure or (2) as an
administrative act toward a teaching elder already under
process ( BCO 31–10). The BCO does not permit a presbytery
to suspend a teaching elder because he requests to be
suspended, nor as the first act of process.
Please excuse our misunderstanding and
lack of clarification on the issues at hand in previous
responses. Palmetto Presbytery did institute process on
February 11, 2021 (minutes submitted), and presbytery acted
to administratively suspend TE [name omitted] (per BCO 3110) from official functions, while presbytery awaited
resolution of the pending civil trial. TE [name omitted]
consented to this administrative suspension.

That the Philadelphia Presbytery direct

Philadelphia Presbytery agrees that we have not yet read and

40-1 requires the Presbytery to review the records of every

Committee recognizes the challenges

important distinction of the Presbyterian Church in America.

responses: From the March 15, 2025 Stated Meeting:
Whereas the PCA General Assembly has cited the
Philadelphia Presbytery for failing to review its Sessions’
Record for the years 2018 and 2020, 2021, and 2023 Whereas
the Book of Church Order ( BCO ) 40-1 requires the Presbytery
to review the records of every session subject to its
jurisdiction yearly, Whereas the Philadelphia undertook a
review of the records of its sessions for the years 2019 and
2023 and that these reviews were made by two persons for
each session and considered matters of form and substance,
The Coordinating Team proposes that it create a committee of
elders to review the session records for the years 2019, 2020,
2021 and 2022 in the following manner: Each Session’s
records for these years would be reviewed by only one
reviewer. The reviewers would focus on identifying possible
instances of gross non-compliance with our constitutional
standards, not on exceptions of form or minor exceptions of
substance which may have been reported in the 2023 review
of Session records. The committee is asked to issue its report
to the Presbytery by October 31, 2025. The committee would

Each Presbytery is required under BCO
40-1 to review all Session minutes at least on a yearly basis.
The continued failure to fully review Session minutes for the
years 2019, 2020, 2021 and 2022 reflects a serious dereliction
of duty. The solution suggested by the Presbytery does not
appear to reflect the intent and spirit of the BCO 40-2 which
requires a thorough examination of Session minutes and not
simply reviewing only for gross non compliance of
constitutional standards.

Presbytery incorrectly judged a candidate’s stated difference as merely semantic. Minute reads: " I would take more of the continental reformers view that part of the Sabbath can be spent in recreation. After church, going to a game, or taking a trip to the mountains, etc.”.

Presbytery incorrectly judged a candidate’s stated differences as merely semantic. First minute read: “I have a perceived difference with the WCF 4.1 (cf. also WLC 15; WSC 9) which states: “It pleased God the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, for the manifestation of the glory of His eternal power, wisdom, and goodness, in the beginning, to create or make of nothing the world, and all things therein, whether visible or invisible, in the space of six days, and all very good.” The Confession here appears to teach that creation took place in the span of six, literal 24 hour periods. I do not believe an orthodox view of creation necessitates the “Calendar Day Interpretation” of Genesis 1-2. That being said, I believe God called all things, both invisible and visible, out of nothing (ex nihilo) into being by his voice, as the Belgic Confession 12 states without reference to the span of time: “We believe that the Father, when it seemed good to him, created heaven and earth and all other creatures from nothing, by the Word—that is to say, by the Son.” Second minute reads: “I have a perceived difference with the WCF 21.8 (cf. also WLC 1117, 119; WSC 60), which states: “This Sabbath is to be kept holy unto the Lord when men, after a due preparing of their hearts, and ordering of their common affairs beforehand, do not only observe an holy rest all the day from their own works, words, and thoughts about their worldly employments and recreations; but also are taken up the whole time in the public and private exercises of His worship, and in the duties of necessity and mercy.” The Confession here appears to teach that personal, private activities for pleasure are not appropriate on the Sabbath. I believe that New Covenant Christians keep the Sabbath holy primarily by worshiping God weekly on the First Day (i.e. Sunday). We rest from our work and ordinary labors in order to facilitate our worship of God. Recreational activities that do not hinder our ability to worship are still permissible. This view is expressed in the Heidelberg Catechism 103: “Q. What is God’s will for you in the fourth commandment? A. First, that the gospel ministry and education for it be maintained, and that, especially on the festive day of rest, I diligently attend the assembly of God’s people to learn what God’s Word teaches, to participate in the sacraments, to pray to God publicly, and to bring Christian offerings for the poor.”” Both statements depart from the Standards’ teaching on the relevant topics, and thus are more than semantic.

Presbytery incorrectly judged a candidate’s stated differences as merely semantic. Both stated differences actually differ from the Confession of Faith. The first difference reads: “I have a perceived difference with the WCF Chapter 21, Section 8: “The Sabbath is then kept holy unto the Lord, when men, after a due preparing of their hearts, and ordering of their affairs beforehand, do not only observe an holy rest, all the day, from their own, works, words, and thoughts about their worldly employments, and recreations, but also are taken up the whole time, in the public and private exercises of his worship, and in the duties of necessity, and mercy.” though i do believe it is important to keep the Sabbath as the Lord’s Day and to worship on the first day of the week, when Christ rose. As the pattern laid out in scripture. I do not think we have to literally rest from all worldly matters to keep this. As humans, we are to set aside a day of the week to worship and rest in the Lord. I think the proper way to observe the Sabbath day is to gather together as the body and worship our God and King. (Matt. 28, Acts 2) I do think it is important to set aside the Lord’s Day to be purposeful in your reflection on God and gather with the body in your local church. This way you hear the Word proclaimed, pray/reflect on the week. And partake of the Lord’s Supper in order to nourish your faith. Though I do not think per the confession, you can do nothing on the Sabbath. I do think doing things with your family, recreation, yardwork, etc. can all be means of rest and bring glory to God. This is part of our living our lives daily as Christians seeking to honor and serve Christ daily. In other words, as Christians we shouldn’t only think about Christ and his word on Sunday. Rather we are to live our daily lives praising him and serving him daily in our various callings. The Sabbath is the day set aside to prepare to live out this calling.” The second difference reads: “I have perceived difference with WCF chapter 4 section 1: “It pleased God the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, and for the manifestation of the glory of his eternal power, wisdom, and goodness in the beginning, to create, or make of nothing, the world, and all things therein whether visible or invisible, in the space of six days; and all very good.” My view of creation would be the standard day age view. The ‘six days’ are understood in the same sense as “in that day” of Isaiah 11:10-11 —that is, as periods of indefinite length and not necessarily of 24 hours duration. There are other similar uses of the Hebrew word for “day” (yôm) in Scripture to support this view of periods longer than 24 hours including that in the very context of Genesis 2:4. Another argument for this approach is that the seventh day in Genesis 1 is not concluded with the boundary phrase, “and there was evening, and there was morning” as with the other days, and therefore it continues, as indicated by Hebrews 4:1-11's quotation of Psalm 95:11.”

actions in the future.

responses: This is the exception to which the General
Assembly exception has been taken: “It is my view that the
Second Commandment’s prohibition against images is always
in the context of worship. Therefore, I believe that images in
a worship context (e.g. stained glass windows or icons
depicting Jesus in a church sanctuary) are a violation of the
Second Commandment. But I do not believe that images of
Jesus used in a pedagogical context (e.g. The Jesus Storybook
Bible or Sunday School curriculum for children) are a
violation of the Second Commandment. I also believe WLC
109’s prohibition against images of Christ “in our mind” goes
further than what Scripture requires and seems to me to be
unrealistic in practice as we read the Gospel accounts in the
New Testament. I believe that to worship any images of the
Triune God (whether external or mental) violates the Second
Commandment.” (Bold emphasis stated clerk’s). Pittsburgh
Presbytery had ruled this was merely semantic. In discussing
the highlighted sentence, presbyters agreed that it does seem
to raise the exception beyond “merely semantic” to the level
of “more than semantic, but not out of accord with any
fundamental of our system of doctrine.” It was m/s/c to adopt
the following response by Pittsburgh Presbytery: Upon further
reflection, the presbytery does agree that the words “goes
further than what the Scripture requires” does move the
exception to the level of “more than semantic, but not out of
accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine,” and
the minutes have been notated accordingly.

description: Presbytery appears to have incorrectly ruled the following stated difference as merely semantic. Candidate appears to deny the spiritual presence of Christ in the Lord’s Supper, holding only to a memorialist view of the sacrament, which is hostile to our system of doctrine. In candidate’s own words: “I do affirm that by the Spirit, Christ is present in and at the observance of the Supper. I believe that the Spirit does confirm and encourage the faith of those who receive the elements by faith in following Jesus in a unique way. We are to remember with love for Christ and sorrow for our sins his body and blood which were sacrificed for us on the cross. But I cannot say that we feed on his spiritual blood or spiritual body in some literal fashion. The act, both physically and spiritually, occurred once and for all time at Golgotha. We rightly counter Roman Catholics who seem repeatedly to crucify Christ when practicing transubstantiation that turns the bread and wine into the corporeal body and blood to feed upon. I am uncomfortable with the Reformed rite if it turns the bread and wine into his spiritual body and spiritual blood upon which we literally feed. The RCC bodily crucifies Christ again and again through the Mass—or so it appears to many Protestants, myself included. I don’t wish to appear to crucify Christ spiritually again and again through a Lord’s Supper that has recipients eating the spiritual body and spiritual blood. When we “feed on him in our hearts by faith with thanksgiving,” I interpret this to mean that we pause to remember and celebrate that at the cross it was finished both in body and in spirit for our salvation—hallelujah and amen. We figuratively feed upon the accomplished work of Christ at the cross as we apply again and again its saving graces to our daily lives through the agency of the supper when accompanied by faith through the Holy Spirit. Again, I may be misunderstanding what the article is saying, in which case, I welcome conversation, and this might not be an exception. For more see the Larger Catechism 168-170 and the Shorter Catechism 96.”.

Presbytery appears to have incorrectly judged that a candidate’s stated difference is not out of accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine because the difference is neither hostile to the system nor strikes at the vitals of religion. “4) WLC #177 (maybe WSC #97). I believe in paedocommunion (young child communion). Our baptized children are ‘not only for the solemn admission of the party baptized into the visible Church, but also to be unto him a sign and seal of the covenant of grace, of his ingrafting into Christ, of regeneration, of remission of sins, and of his giving up unto God, through Jesus Christ, to walk in newness of life’ ( WCF 28.1). I believe they should likewise be welcome at the Lord’s Table as part of this family when they are welcomed to the kitchen table as part of their families (i.e., not as suckling infants but as young children). As described in the WCF 31.4, ‘suspension from the sacrament of the Lord’s Supper for a season’ is for those who are being disciplined, and I do not believe it is biblical to treat, our covenant children the same way as 89 ‘obstinate offenders.’ ( WCF 31.3) I agree with Dr. Robert S. Rayburn’s position in the Minority Report to the Ad-Interim Committee to Study the Question of Paedocommunion (16th GA). However, and this is probably the most important aspect of this, i will not nor have i ever practiced anything contrary to that which is permitted in the Westminster Standards and the BCO, as God’s Word and my ordination vows would require me to submit in practice to the judgment of the church in this matter.” ["I believe in paedocommunion."].

Presbytery appears to have incorrectly judged that a candidate’s stated difference is not out of accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine because the difference is neither hostile to the system nor strikes at the vitals of religion. TE seems to imply that making images of God isn’t forbidden by the second commandment. “2) Larger Catechism 109. Where the Confession states ‘The sins forbidden in the second commandment are…the making any representation of God, or of any of the three persons, either inwardly in our mind, or outwardly in any kind of image or likeness of any creature whatsoever,’ I believe that Scripture teaches in the second commandment (Exodus 20:4) that it is forbidden to make idols or images to bow down and worship, but not that it is forbidden to make any representation of God at all, either inwardly or outwardly. Though Christian tradition has generally found it impious to create images of God the Father, it has not generally thought the same with regard to Christ, the Son of God, or the Holy Spirit. More specifically, the Scriptures themselves speak of the form of Jesus (e.g. Isaiah 53, John 20:24- 29, etc.) and of the Holy Spirit in the form of a creature (Matthew 3:16). Thus I believe that the confession goes beyond the prohibitions of Scripture in this regard.”

description: Presbytery adopted a policy requiring honorably retired ministers to transfer their membership upon relocation to another Presbytery. responses: In correspondence with the Stated Clerk’s
office, we adopted a policy that stated it is up to the will of the
Presbytery body to determine if a candidate should remain in
our Presbytery, even if honorably retired. In this case, we had
a retired minister who lived 2,000 miles away, making
repeated requests of Presbytery even though he had no
involvement in our body. We are willing to modify our
language or rescind the policy if it violates the will of RPR.
BCO 13-5 does not apply to an honorably
retired TE.
At our January 2025 Stated Meeting, we
will rescind this change from our standing rules. Once the
motion carries, I will attach the January 2025 minutes to
reflect the change.

responses: The incoming applicant for licensure, an
ordained PCA TE from another Presbytery, was seeking to
serve as a stated supply fora particular church within the
Presbytery and was therefore examined for licensure (per
BCO 19-1, 19-2). He did not submit stated differences.
However, in the course of his licensure examination, both in
the committee and on the floor, touching upon his views in
theology, it became clear in the judgment of the Presbytery
that his view of the acceptability of liturgical dance in worship
did not conform to the teaching of WCF 21. In response to
extra-confessional views orally expressed by the applicant on
the floor, the Presbytery took action to restrict his teaching
concerning the elements acceptable in worship, so that it
conforms to WCF 21.3-5 as long as he’s engaged in the
ministry of the word within the bounds of our Presbytery.
While the Presbytery did examine and probe the applicant’s
views beyond written materials provided, and while the
Presbytery did take measures to seek to guard the confessional
integrity of the teaching within her bounds, we do
acknowledge our failure to record any differences orally
expressed in the applicant’s own words. We will be more
diligent to document any differences discovered during
exams, and we will be careful to record such matters more
thoroughly.
No record that Presbytery corrected its
actions ( RAO 16-10.b.1). Presbytery has not submitted the
candidate’s stated differences in his own words for review.
I was introduced to liturgical dance at
[Church Name Omitted], Birmingham, Alabama, in a preGeneral Assembly evening worship service in the 1980s.
Liturgical dance in the art form of ballet is the use of the Godcreated body to express the beauty, majesty, grace and
holiness of God. On the basis of 4 passages in Scripture that
either describe or encourage worshipful dance, I believe that
when truly used as a doxological form, liturgical dance falls
within the regulative principle of worship.

this new response separately to the RPR committee, which

This response was taken up on the floor of the RPR
committee meeting, along with responses to other exceptions
that had already been submitted, and all of them except for
one were recommended to be found satisfactory, which is the
action the 51st GA took (please see 51st GA minutes, pages
We will be careful in the future to submit our responses in a

[name omitted] as an intern of the Presbytery according to
BCO 19-7 and 19-10. The nature of Mr. [name omitted]’s
internship, and how it would fulfill the BCO 19-7 (para 3),
was discussed at the Committee level and widely known
among the body, but we failed to articulate it clearly on the
floor of Presbytery. The prayer and address of BCO 19-10 was
properly undertaken at Presbytery, but not properly recorded
in the minutes. We will correct these omissions, and we will
endeavor in the future to both carefully attend to the
requirements for establishing interns and the recording of the
process in our minutes.

description: Presbytery granted a doctrinal exception that appears to be out of accord with the fundamentals of our doctrinal standards. This statement seems to say we can make “images” that picture the incarnate second person of the Trinity, even “placing them in a worship space,” so long as they are not made an object of worship.
responses: At a prior stated meeting of the Presbytery
(September 22, 2022), the transfer applicant submitted the
following stated difference with WLC 109, which the
Presbytery ruled as “not out of accord with any fundamental
of our system of doctrine” [per RAO 16-3.e.5.c]
WLC 109 – I do not believe the making of any
representation of the Son of God is prohibited by Scripture as
the Divines state. Specifically, I do not believe Scripture
prohibits representations of Jesus for educational or artistic
purposes. The incarnation raises questions about the
representation of the Son of God which cannot adequately be
answered simply by an appeal to passages like Deuteronomy
4:15-19 (since in the case of Jesus the point would be
precisely that God did appear in the “likeness of sinful flesh,”
to quote Paul). Moreover, the second commandment has
reference to the narrow issue of worshipping by means of any
image of any created thing, not simply the making of images
per se. On several occasions God actually commands the
making of images, often to be placed in the very space in
which worship occurs (Ex 25:18, 31-33; 28:33; 1 Kgs 7:29; 2
Chr 4:3-4). Thus, the prohibition in view is not the making of
images, or even placing them in a worship space, but more
narrowly the use of any image as the object of worship.
The applicant submitted his exception, as pertaining
only to the prohibition of the making images of created things,
citing several instances in the Scriptures where images of
created things were placed in the temple. Since then, he, now
as a member of this Presbytery, acknowledged the poor
wording in his original stated difference that led to confusion
and clarified that his stated difference does not extend to the
image of Jesus being “placed in a worship space”.
He submitted the following clarification:
“I don’t have any objection to images of CREATED
things in the worship space for decorative purposes or to
create a worship aesthetic as was done in both tabernacle and
temple, and most church buildings I would think. I think RPR
misunderstood me to be saying that I thought it was therefore
okay to put an image of JESUS in the worship space. I actually
don't think that’s wise as it could encourage the use of an
image of Jesus as an object of worship or veneration, and it
distracts from the central place of the Word in worship ... I
don’t believe images of Jesus should be placed in the worship
space, and that my stated difference had to do merely with
representations of Jesus for artistic or educational purposes.”
At the 132nd stated meeting on January 24th, 2025,
the Presbytery judged this to be more than semantic, but not
out of accord with any fundamental of our system of doctrine.

Jan 25, 2022- TE [Name Omitted]-
WCF 21.2 WCF 21.8, WLC 117, WSC 60- I do not
believe that Scripture requires. Sabbath rest from “words and
thoughts about their worldly employments and recreations.” I
believe that God made the Sabbath for man (Mk. 2:27) to
teach us to consecrate our earthly work, vocations, and
enjoyments to him (1 Cor. 10:31). Scripture seems to teach
that rest is the ultimate end of the Sabbath and that man, being
made in the image of God, is to remember our salvation in
him and to enjoy the benefits of our redemption in him
through acts of mercy, fellowship with the body, and
enjoyment of his creation on the Lord’s day.
WLC 109- In forbidding the “making any
representation of God, of all or any of the three persons, either
inwardly in our mind, or outwardly in any kind of image or
likeness of any creature whatsoever,” i believe the l cis
taking the second commandment too literally, overlooking the
intent of the command, which is to love and fear God above
all else. In our reading of Scripture, the prologue of John,
Colossians 1, or Revelation 19, for example, it seems
impossible not to imagine Christ in our mind. I believe that
the 2nd commandment teaches us not to wrongly ascribe
worship to something that is not God, or in the case of the
Golden calf in Ex. 32-34, to wrongly ascribe worship to a
graven image, even if that image is attempting to capture the
triune God’s nature. Also, the confession would seem to
forbid the making of any symbol ascribed to God worthy of
worship, such as a cross. I do not believe this is the intent of
the 2nd commandment. That being said, i do believe it is
wrong to attempt to contain the first and third members of the
Trinity with imagery since they are invisible in substance.
WCF 21.5- I take singing of psalms to mean the
hymnody of the Psalter and non-Scriptural spiritual songs and
hymns as the Apostle Paul means in Eph. 5:19. CHAR (10)
WCF 24.6- I believe there may be otherwise and apt reasons
that align with the teachings of Scripture for a marriage to end.
In the cases of abuse, i believe this to be a violation of the 1st,
5th, and 6th commandments. Particularly, in the case of
repeated abuse, it may be un-biblical for the marriage to
continue, especially after church discipline has been carried
out and the abuser remains un-repentant. In this case, the
abusing spouse has neglected their duty to their family and
according to Eph. 5-6 and violated their marriage covenant. In
this case, I believe divorce is permissible. There may also be
cases where trust is broken repeatedly by violating the 8th,
9th, and 10th commandments within the marriage and family
that the same principles above are necessary. It also seems
appropriate to note that if one spouse apostatizes from the
faith, divorce may be permissible according to 1 Cor. 7. In
these cases, divorce may be advised to a member by the
session or presbytery.
April 26, 2022- TE [Name Omitted]-
It is with gratitude and conviction that I affirm my
subscription to the Westminster Confession of Faith and
Catechisms as containing the system of doctrine taught in the
Holy Scriptures.
In the interest of full candor, I make you aware of the
following:
1. WCF 21.8 insists that that one’s actions on the
Sabbath “are taken up, the whole time, in the public and
private exercises of his worship.” It seems the Standards say
more about worship and duties and less about rest than do the
Scriptures. Public and private worship must be maintained on
the Lord’s Day as well as other duties of religion, yet to do
them “all the day” and “the whole time” would seem in
tension with the emphasis Scripture places on rest. While
avoiding the idolatry of our commercial culture, which never
pauses for rest or reflection, I believe restorative and recreative activities are not forbidden on the Lord’s Day and can
be understood as fulfilling the requirement of “rest.”
Furthermore, with the finished work of Jesus Christ manifest
in the resurrection from the dead, the New Covenant
community gathers for worship on the first day of the week
now known as “the Lord’s Day” (Rev 1:10). Though not in
tension with the Old Testament Sabbath, the New Covenant
terminology seems more appropriate.
2. WLC 109 forbids the “making any representation
of God...either inwardly in our mind, or outwardly....” Images
of the Triune God which are intentionally or unintentionally
produced or kept as a means of aiding worship (especially in
the sanctuary where such worship is ordinarily conducted) are
disallowed. The second commandment forbids fashioning any
image which believers might bow down to or offer any form
or part of worship. At the same time, in forbidding all mental
representations of God in all contexts and for all purposes,
seems to require what is impossible.
3. I hold that God has chosen to relate with his people
through one overarching covenant of grace, which remains
constant throughout history and that, as the people of God,
Israel and the Church share in the same promises. At the same
time, I hold that on the last day, at the one return of Jesus
Christ, both those found alive along with the redeemed dead
will be raised to life, yet at a later time the unredeemed dead
with be raised for judgment. Because of statements in
Revelation 20:4-5, I understand that WLC 87-88, which
speaks of the “general resurrection of the dead, both of the just
and the unjust,” may be temporally separate events. Though
much of the language in Revelation is symbolic, the
description of two resurrections in Revelation 20 lead met oa
“historic premillennial” position. Citation J. Gresham
Machen (“Premillennialism,” The Presbyterian Guardian 3/2
[24 October 1936], 45) argues:
“Subscription to the Westminster Standards in the
Presbyterian Church of America [e.g., OPC] is not to every
word in those Standards, but only to the system of doctrine
which the Standards contain. The real question, then, is
whether a person who holds the Premillennial view can hold
that system. Can a person who holds the Premillennial view
be a true Calvinist; can he, in other words, hold truly to the
Calvinistic or Reformed system of doctrine which is set forth
in the Westminster Standards? We think that he can; and for
that reason we think that Premillennialists as well as those
who hold the opposing view may become ministers or elders
or deacons in The Presbyterian Church of America. We think
that a man who holds that the return of Christ and the final
judgment take place not in one act, as the Westminster
Standards contemplate them as doing, but in two acts with a
thousand-year reign of Christ upon the earth in between, yet
may honestly say that he holds the system of doctrine that the
Standards contain.”
Sept 27, 2022- TE [Name Omitted]-
I take one exception to the Westminster Confession
of Faith on the issue of recreation on the Sabbath. WCF
Section 21.8 reads:
This Sabbath is then kept holy unto the Lord, when men, after
a due preparing of their hearts, and ordering of their common
affairs beforehand, do not only observe an holy rest all the day
from their own works, words, and thoughts, about their
worldly employments and recreations; but also are taken
up the whole time in the public and private exercises of his
worship, and in the duties of necessity and mercy.
I believe that the intent of the Scriptures regarding the
Sabbath is not to prevent recreational bodily activity that
might further our rest on Sabbath and enjoyment of the Lord.
The Confession grounds its reasoning for disallowing
recreation on a misreading of the words “doing your pleasure”
found in Isaiah 58:13. “Doing your pleasure” in the context of
Isaiah is referencing keeping one’s business open or not
letting their fields rest. It has to do with commercial activity
and not recreational activity. Thus, while our time in public
and private worship ought to orient the way we spend our time
on the Sabbath, I see nothing in the Scriptures which would
prevent a christian from lawfully taking a walk, hike, or
engaging in any other refreshing recreational activity,
especially when in the company of fellow saints.

description: Presbytery implicitly granted a doctrinal exception that needs further clarity for proper review. Mr. [name omitted] appears to advocate for paedo-communion ( WLC 177).
responses: Presbytery regrets this lack of clarity in
full recordkeeping. It is correct that Mr. [Name Omitted] gave
a stated difference with the Westminster Standards in line
with what is called “paedocommunion” (though Mr. [Name
Omitted] did not use this term). The Presbytery questioned
him thoroughly about this view, especially as it touched other
doctrines in our confessional system and “Federal Vision
theology.” The Presbytery was satisfied with his answers,
especially his commitment not to promote this view or
practice it. Since the Presbytery has allowed for this exception
in the past, it was deemed acceptable still. We will strive to
record the full extent of the Presbytery’s examination of its
candidates’ stated differences with the Standards.
Presbytery needs to demonstrate how his
views are not hostile to our system of doctrine.
Presbytery judged this particular
exception from this particular candidate as a difference more
than semantic but not hostile to our system or striking at the
vitals of religion for a number of reasons. The candidate
affirmed that his view came from within the PCA, especially
from reading the minority report of Robert Rayburn, who
remained a minister in good standing in the denomination. His
difference was not influenced by disagreement with other
fundamentals of our system—e.g., justification by faith alone,
the efficacious reception of the sacraments as dependent upon
personal faith, and the obligation of individual selfexamination. Further, the candidate promised to submit to the
BCO requirements and not practice his view. He expressed his
desire to maintain peace and unity and would not cause
dissension on this topic. Presbytery argued in accordance with
the denomination’s prior allowance of this exception in this
manner (not practicing or pushing it). On a similar issue RPR
previously referred to BCO 34-5, which allows for such
differences if they are not “industriously spread” and “are not
likely to do much injury” (43rd General Assembly, pp. 422–
23). Presbytery believes such pertains in this case.

description: It appears committees are empowered to act as if they were commissions.
responses: South Texas Presbytery concurs with the
Exception and regrets the oversite. RE [Name Omitted]’s
report included only one Committee item which needed
Presbytery approval, and because there was no quorum, a
motion and second was required. Our minutes were unclear
on that point. Our Standing Rules empowers the Ministerial
Relations Committee to act as a commission to dissolve the
pastoral relations of a minister in cases “where the minister
and congregation formally concur in such dissolution and
when pastoral problems are not involved.” The Chairman of
the committee should have asked for Presbytery ratification of
the committee/commission’s actions. As for the report of the
Administrative Committee on the complaint against TE
[Name Omitted], the Standing Rules grant the Committee
authority for initial investigation to determine if the complaint
was in order. This is what happened. The Presbytery regrets
the ambiguity of its minutes. in the case of TE [Name
Omitted]’s theology and exegetical papers, the Stated Clerk
failed to memorialize the motion of the Candidates and
Credentials Committee’s motion to approve those actions.
The Presbytery regrets this oversite and will endeavor to be
more complete in future records keeping.
There is no record that the relevant
minutes have been corrected.
In the case of the April meeting, the
Nominations Committee was not acting as a commission. The
reference to "two items of business" that may have given thie
improssion included one item that required a presbytery vote,
given the committee lacked a quorum, and the presbytery
voted. The second item was an announcement. The Presbytery
therefore does not feel a correction to the minutes is needed.
At the April meeting, the Ministerial Relations Committee
under the Presbytery Standing Rules adopted by the full
Presbytery "has the authority to act as a commission... in
dissolving pastoral relationships... where the minister and
congregation formally concur in such dissolution and ...
pastoral problems are not involved." The committee was
acting in this capacity, the minutes have been corrected to
reflect this, and the Stated Clerk has been authorized to make
a marginal notation in the minutes indicating this correction.
The questions around the Administrative Committee
actions at the October meeting are addressed in the response
to Exception 76-2023-13.

description: Minutes provide an incomplete record of cases of discipline, and complaints received. Minutes indicate commission received confession of a TE and imposed censures of deposition from office and indefinite suspension from the sacraments. This may have been a BCO 38-1 case without process but there is no indication; it appears from the AC report that this commission was retroactively authorized and therefore had no authority to conduct the case or impose censure; also no indication procedures in BCO 36-7 were followed; also not clear if accused was a church officer; confession is not in minutes.
responses: South Texas Presbytery concurs with the
Exception and regrets its error. This was a case without
process. The Presbytery erred in its initial handling the case
and these errors were reported by the Administrative
Committee to the floor of Presbytery. The Presbytery chose to
ratify the actions of the judicial commission and the case
continued to the deposition of Mr. [Name Omitted]. The
recitation required by BCO 36-7 was read to Mr. [Name
Omitted], but the Clerk failed to note that in the minutes of
the Presbytery. Mr. [Name Omitted] then chose to associate
with another branch of the Church, severing his relationship
with the PCA. The Presbytery will endeavor to avoid such
irregularities in exercising discipline in the future.
There is no record that the relevant
minutes have been corrected or that the appropriate
documents related to the 38-1 matter have been attached to the
minutes.
As noted in the Presbytery's response to
the 50th General Assembly, the Presbytery confirms that the
matter involving Teaching Elder [Name Omitted] was a case
without process under Section 38-1 of the Book of Church
Order. The Presbytery acknowledges that errors were made in
the proceeding, including in the establishment of a judicial
commission under Chapter 15, as is reflected in the minutes
of the Presbytery. The Session received the confession of
[Name Omitted], and a copy will be maintained in the records
of the Presbytery. The Presbytery also affirms that the censure
of deposition was administered to [Name Omitted] in
accordance with Section 36-7. As is noted in the minutes and
the response, [Name Omitted] subsequently associated with
another branch of the Church. The minutes of the Presbytery
have been corrected to reflect this response, and the stated
clerk has been authorized by the Presbytery to make a
marginal notation in the minutes indicating this correction.

Omitted] in [Name Omitted] was reviewed by the Candidates
and Credentials Committee and presented to the Presbytery,
found in good order, deemed for the good of the Church under
Section 46-6 of the Book of Church Order, and placed in
Weller's hands, who found it acceptable, and the Stated Clerk
has been authorized to make a marginal notation in the
minutes indicating this correction.

description: Minutes of commission not entered in Presbytery minutes, and these must be presented for review next year.
responses: Minutes of the commissions to ordain
and/or install Teaching Elders [Names Omitted] were entered
ni the Presbytery minutes in 2024.

description: No record of annual reports received on every intern and on candidates.
responses: The requirement for annual reports to the
Presbytery from all interns and candidates was not satisfied in
2023 and the presbytery feels it is impracticable and
unrealistic to seek to secure such reports at this time. Annual
reports to and reports at each stated meeting of the Presbytery
were presented in 2024. In addition, the directory has been
corrected to accurately reflect all interns and candidates.

description: No record of receiving reports from teaching elders working outside the bounds, and no record of Presbytery approval of TE laboring outside bounds.
responses: The requirement for annual reports to the
Presbytery from all teaching elders laboring out of bounds
was not satisfied in 2023 and the Presbytery feels it is
impracticable and unrealisticto seek to secure such reports at
this time. Annual reports were presented in 2024. In addition,
the directory has been corrected to accurately reflect the status
of all Teaching Elders laboring out of bounds.

description: All specific requirements of licensure exam not recorded.
responses: The minutes have been corrected to reflect
that the examination of [Name Omitted] on January 28 on the
Book of Church Order encompassed knowledge of the
government of the Presbyterian Church in America as
outlined in the BCO and that the examination of his Christian
call and experience on August 13, 2022 included his
inwardcall to preach the Gospel such that, with the recitation
of his fulfillment of the other requirements recorded in the
January 2023 minutes, all requirements for his licensure had
been completed, and the Stated Clerk has been authorized to
make a marginal notation in the minutes indicating this
correction.

description: Incomplete record of ordination exam.
responses: The minutes have been corrected to reflect
that all requirements of the ordination examination of [Name
Omitted] to the office of minister were properly sustained in
accordance with the Book of Church Order. These
requirements included: (i) Demonstration of a sufficient
knowledge of the Greek and Hebrew languages, the
Candidates and Credentials Committee having reviewed and
accepted the man’s seminary studies wherein these subjects
were included; (ii) Preaching of a sermon before the
Credentials Committee of the Presbytery on April 30, 2022;
(iii) Preparation of exegetical and theological papers,
reviewed and accepted by the Candidates and Credentials
Committee on January 28, 2023; and (iv) Satisfaction of
examinations of his Christian experience (conducted prior to
the acceptance of his transfer from the Central Carolina
Presbytery on April 30, 2022), and his knowledge of the Book
of Church Order and the government and discipline of the
Church (sustained at the April 30, 2022 Stated Meeting, as
noted in the minutes). The Stated Clerk has been authorized
to make a marginal notation in the minutes indicating this
correction.

description: Incomplete record of ordination exam.
responses: The minutes have been correctedto reflect
that al requirements ofthe ordination examination of [Name
Omitted] to the office of Teaching Elder under Section 21-4
of the Book of Church Order had been satisfied, including
specifically fulfillment of the requirement for knowledgeof
the Greekand Hebrew languages (which was subsumed ni the
statement ni the minutes that "... [Name Omitted] has
completedal the requirements for ordination", the Candidates
and Credentials Committee having reviewed and accepted on
April 28, 2023 histheological studies that included these
subjects), and theStated Clerk hasbeen authorized to make a
marginalnotation in the minutes indicating this correction.

description: Meeting attendance roll list church with less than 350 members with three or more ruling elder commissioners present with no record of which two are voting.
responses: The commissioners from [Church Name
Omitted] and [Church Name Omitted] eligible to vote on
behalf of their sessions have been identified. The minutes
have been corrected and notated. Beginning with the August
2024 Stated Meeting, the attendance roll distinguishes
between ruling elder commissioners and other ruling elders
attending the meeting.

description: — The Ministerial Relations Committee acted as a commission without a quorum.
responses: The Ministerial Relations Committee
under the Presbytery Standing Rules adopted by the full
Presbytery "has the authority to act as a commission . . . in
dissolving pastoral relationships . . . where the minister and
congregation formally concurn in such dissolution
and...pastoral problems are not involved." The Presbytery has
received minutes of the electronic votes taken by the
committee, as is permitted under the Standing Rules adopted
by the full Presbytery, on May 10 and June 23, 2023 with
respect to Teaching Elders [Name Omitted] and [Name
Omitted]. The minutes of the Presbytery Stated Meeting on
August 12 have been corrected to note these minutes and the
Stated Clerk authorized to make a notation of this correction.
No separate minutes or the August 11 committee meeting and
the action with respect to Teaching Elder [Name Omitted]
were kept and the committee report of its action relled on the
provision of the Standing Rules. (There is no record in the
minutes of the absence of a quorum for this committee
meeting.)

description: No record of annual report(s) of TE(s)laboring out of bounds.
No record of annual report(s) of TE(s) serving as chaplains.
Annual reports of candidates not included.
Annual report of interns not included.
No record of endorsement by candidate's Session.
No record of sixmonths membership for candidate.
No record of endorsements by sessions of men coming under care.
No record of sixmonths membership for candidate.
No record of requiring statement of differences with our Standards.
Stated difference not judged with the prescribed categories.
No record that the congregation (/Session) was cited to appear before the presbytery.
No record of six-months membership for candidate.
responses: We agree with the exception. In the future,
we will add the following language to our minutes, “[This
candidate], having met the requirements of BCO 18-2 and

was received under care of SEAL Presbytery, as moved by the
Committee and approved by Presbytery.”

have been corrected.

Presbytery, the previously adopted minutes were corrected
with the following language: "Mr. [Names Omitted], having
met the requirements of BCO 18-2 and having been
examined/questioned in accord with BCO 18-3, was received
under care of SEAL Presbytery, as moved by the Committee
and approved by Presbytery."

description: No record of examinations (Under Care / Internship).
responses: We agree with the exception. In the future,
we will add the following language to our minutes, “[This
candidate], having met the requirements of BCO 18-2 and
having been examined/questioned in accord with BCO 18-3
was received under care of SEAL Presbytery, as moved by the
Committee and approved by Presbytery.”
Presbytery must report that the minutes
have been corrected.
At the October 22, 2024 meeting of
Presbytery, the previously adopted minutes were corrected
with the following language: "Mr. [Names Omitted], having
met the requirements of BCO 18-2 and having been
examined/questioned in accord with BCO 18-3, was received
under care of SEAL Presbytery, as moved by the Committee
and approved by Presbytery."

description: Presbytery did not make record of the trial parts omitted in an extraordinary case of licensure.
responses: At the October 22, 2024 meeting of
Presbytery, the previously adopted minutes were corrected
with the following language: "All portions of BCO 19.2.a-f.
were omitted in the licensure of Dr. [Name Omitted]."

description: The portion of the call for the meeting stating the purpose of the meeting is not recorded verbatim in the minutes of the meeting.
responses: We understand this exception. The stated
purpose of the meeting was included in Appendix B. In the
future, we will include this language in the minutes
themselves.

description: The portion of the call for the meeting stating the purpose of the meeting is not recorded verbatim in the minutes of the meeting.
responses: We understand this exception. The stated
purpose of the meeting was included in Appendix B. In the
future, we will include this language in the minutes
themselves.

description: No record of 60-days notice of dissolution.
responses: [Church Name Omitted] petitioned
Presbytery for its dissolution at the October 25, 2022 meeting.
The action taken by Presbytery at the January 24, 2023
meeting was the fulfillment of the request after 60-days
notice.
This was our Presbytery's response to 2023-14, which was
found satisfactory by the 51st General Assembly: "We agree
to this exception. This action was in response to a request
received from [Name Omitted] PCA on October 24, 2022. We
took final action at our next stated meeting, and the property
and members were transferred to the care of the Session of
[Name Omitted] PCA. We failed to include this
communication in our minutes/records. We will correct our
actions in the future."

description: No record of endorsement by candidate’s Session.
responses: Presbytery noted that the requirements of
BCO 18-2 were fulfilled for this candidate. Historically, the
documentation of those requirements has not been recorded in
Presbytery's minutes. To correct all previous records of
Presbytery in this regard would be unnecessarily burdensome,
when we believe this language is sufficient.

description: No record of sixmonths membership for candidate.
responses: Presbytery noted that the requirements of
BCO 18-2 were fulfilled for this candidate. Historically, the
documentation of those requirements has not been recorded in
Presbytery's minutes. To correct all previous records of
Presbytery in this regard would be unnecessarily burdensome,
when we believe this language is sufficient.

description: No record of commission proceedings/minutes.
responses: We agree with this exception. We will
include the minutes of this Commission in the minutes of our
October 22, 2024 minutes as an Appendix.

description: Stated differences not recorded in the minister’s/candidate’s own words.
responses: This candidate had no stated differences
with our standards. BCO 21-4 does not seem to require such a
statement on the part of the candidate or of Presbytery if such
differences do not exist. If desired, Presbytery can add the
following language in the future: "This candidate had no
stated differences with our standards."

description: No record of requiring statement of differences with our Standards.
responses: This candidate had no stated differences
with our standards. BCO 21-4 does not seem to require such a
statement on the part of the candidate or of Presbytery if such
differences do not exist. If desired, Presbytery can add the
following language in the future: "This candidate had no

description: No record of requiring statement of differences with our Standards.
responses: This candidate had no stated differences
with our standards. BCO 21-4 does not seem to require such a
statement on the part of the candidate or of Presbytery if such
differences do not exist. If desired, Presbytery can add the
following language in the future: "This candidate had no

description: No record of requiring statement of differences with our Standards.
responses: This candidate had no stated differences
with our standards. BCO 21-4 does not seem to require such a
statement on the part of the candidate or of Presbytery if such
differences do not exist. If desired, Presbytery can add the
following language in the future: "This candidate had no
